title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Install development helper packages R: Install package dependencies (randomForestSRC >= 3.4.0 required):
code: install.packages(c(   "devtools",    # load, test, check, document in one place   "testthat",    # testing framework   "roxygen2",    # builds man/ pages from inline comments   "lintr",       # style linter   "covr",        # test coverage measurement   "pkgdown"      # builds the website )) install.packages(c("randomForestSRC", "randomForest", "ggplot2",                    "dplyr", "tidyr", "survival"))

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Open ggRandomForests.Rproj RStudio/Positron. load package development mode — makes functions available without installing: Confirm works:
code: # Fork the repo on GitHub first, then: git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/ggRandomForests.git cd ggRandomForests  # Add the upstream remote so you can pull future changes git remote add upstream https://github.com/ehrlinger/ggRandomForests.git devtools::load_all()   # shortcut: Ctrl+Shift+L library(randomForestSRC) rf <- rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris) plot(gg_error(rf))

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Key rule: man/ NAMESPACE files always auto-generated. Run devtools::document() Roxygen change commit updated files.
code: R/                   All source code — one file per function family   gg_*.R             Data extraction functions (return gg_* objects)   plot.gg_*.R        S3 plot methods (return ggplot objects)   help.R             Package-level ?ggRandomForests documentation   zzz.R              .onAttach startup message  man/                 Auto-generated — never edit by hand   *.Rd               Built from Roxygen comments by devtools::document()  tests/   testthat/     test_*.R         One test file per R/ source file  vignettes/   ggRandomForests.qmd  Main package vignette (Quarto)  DESCRIPTION          Package metadata, dependencies, version NAMESPACE            Exports/imports — auto-generated by roxygen2 NEWS.md              Changelog — add an entry for every user-visible change

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Every feature package follows two-step pattern: two steps? Keeping data plotting separate means users can inspect, save, transform, combine intermediate data plotting, apply ggplot2 layers want top returned object.
code: forest object     │     ▼ gg_*(forest)          ← R/gg_*.R   — data extraction, returns a gg_* data.frame     │     ▼ plot(gg_object)       ← R/plot.gg_*.R — builds and returns a ggplot2 object

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: gg_* object just data.frame extra class attributes: extra class ("gg_vimp") lets R dispatch plot(gg_dta) plot.gg_vimp automatically R’s S3 system.
code: # Example: what gg_vimp returns class(gg_vimp(rf)) # [1] "gg_vimp"     "data.frame"

what: S3 dispatch for multiple forest packages
title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: gg_* functions support randomForestSRC randomForest objects. pattern : methods return identically structured gg_* object plot.gg_vimp works either.
code: # 1. Generic — dispatches based on class of `object` gg_vimp <- function(object, ...) {   UseMethod("gg_vimp", object) }  # 2. rfsrc method gg_vimp.rfsrc <- function(object, ...) { ... }  # 3. randomForest method gg_vimp.randomForest <- function(object, ...) { ... }

what: Example: adding a new gg_* function
title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Suppose want add gg_depth() plot average tree depth. skeleton: create R/plot.gg_depth.R following pattern plot.gg_error.R.
code: # R/gg_depth.R  #' Tree depth data object #' #' Extracts average depth statistics per tree from a random forest. #' #' @param object A fitted \code{\link[randomForestSRC]{rfsrc}} or #'   \code{\link[randomForest]{randomForest}} object. #' @param ... Optional arguments passed to methods. #' #' @return A \code{gg_depth} \code{data.frame} with columns \code{ntree} #'   and \code{depth}. #' #' @seealso \code{\link{plot.gg_depth}} #' #' @examples #' rf <- rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris) #' plot(gg_depth(rf)) #' #' @export gg_depth <- function(object, ...) {   UseMethod("gg_depth", object) }  #' @export gg_depth.rfsrc <- function(object, ...) {   # ... extract depth data ...   gg_dta <- data.frame(ntree = seq_len(object$ntree), depth = depths)   class(gg_dta) <- c("gg_depth", class(gg_dta))   invisible(gg_dta) }

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Always work branch — never commit directly main: development cycle :
code: git checkout -b my-feature-name devtools::load_all()       # reload after editing source devtools::test()           # run tests devtools::document()       # rebuild man/ from Roxygen comments devtools::check()          # full R CMD check (slow — run before PR)

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Tests live tests/testthat/ named test_<source_file>.R match file cover. framework testthat.

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
code: # tests/testthat/test_gg_depth.R test_that("gg_depth returns correct class for rfsrc", {   rf <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50)    gg_dta <- gg_depth(rf)    expect_s3_class(gg_dta, "gg_depth")   expect_s3_class(gg_dta, "data.frame")   expect_true(all(c("ntree", "depth") %in% names(gg_dta)))   expect_equal(nrow(gg_dta), rf$ntree) })  test_that("plot.gg_depth returns a ggplot", {   rf <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50)    gg_plt <- plot(gg_depth(rf))    expect_s3_class(gg_plt, "ggplot") })  test_that("gg_depth throws on wrong input", {   expect_error(gg_depth("not a forest")) })

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Keep forests small tests — ntree = 50 plenty, faster default 1000. Test error path well happy path (expect_error, expect_warning). Use expect_s3_class() rather older expect_is(). Avoid set.seed() unless explicitly testing something random — randomForestSRC results stochastic exact-value tests break across versions. Run tests single file development: Check coverage (aim > 80%):
code: testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test_gg_depth.R") covr::package_coverage()

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Documentation written Roxygen2 comments (lines starting #') immediately function.

what: Required sections for every exported function
title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
code: #' Short one-line title #' #' One or two paragraphs describing what the function does and why. #' #' @param arg1 Type and meaning. Include the default value if there is one. #' @param arg2 ... #' #' @return Describe what is returned: the class, the columns in any #'   data.frame, and any class attributes set on the object. #' #' @seealso \code{\link{related_function}} #' #' @examples #' # A runnable example — must complete in < 10 seconds for CRAN #' rf <- rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50) #' plot(gg_something(rf)) #' #' @export

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: @param every argument, including ... extras meaningful. @return must describe shape output — just class name. @seealso links paired plot.* function (gg_* function plot.* file). @examples must runnable without error R CMD check. Wrap slow examples \donttest{}. Never wrap \dontrun{} unless literally run CRAN (network, credentials, etc.). Internal helpers (exported) get @keywords internal instead @export. Rebuild docs change: spot-check result:
code: devtools::document() ?gg_depth

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Every user-visible change needs bullet NEWS.md appropriate version heading:
code: ggRandomForests v2.7.0 ===================== * Add `gg_depth()` to visualise average tree depth per forest (#42)

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: package follows tidyverse style guide. Key points: Check code lintr opening PR: Common issues lintr flags: Lines > 120 characters. T / F instead TRUE / FALSE. Trailing whitespace. 1:n instead seq_len(n). inherits(x, "cls") == FALSE instead !inherits(x, "cls").
code: lintr::lint_package()

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: opening PR, run checks CI runs: clean check means: One note package size installed path acceptable. Errors warnings must fixed PR can merged. reproduce exact CI matrix locally can use rhub:
code: # Quick: just tests devtools::test()  # Thorough: full R CMD check (builds vignette, checks examples, etc.) devtools::check() 0 errors ✔ | 0 warnings ✔ | 0 notes ✔ rhub::rhub_check()

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Commit changes clear, present-tense message: Push fork: Open PR GitHub main branch ehrlinger/ggRandomForests. PR description checklist — include description: problem solve feature add? functions new changed? add update tests? add NEWS.md entry? devtools::check() pass cleanly? CI run automatically across macOS, Windows, Linux R release, devel, oldrel-1. checks must pass merge.
code: git add R/gg_depth.R R/plot.gg_depth.R tests/testthat/test_gg_depth.R git commit -m "Add gg_depth() for average tree depth visualisation" git push origin my-feature-name

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Avoid “WIP”, “fix”, “update” context.
code: Add gg_depth() for average tree depth               ← new feature Fix factor ordering in gg_partial categorical branch ← bug fix Improve @return docs for gg_rfsrc                   ← documentation Refactor bootstrap_survival to utils.R              ← refactor

title: Contributing to ggRandomForests
text: Bug reports feature requests: GitHub Issues. Search existing issues filing new one. Questions usage: GitHub Discussions post Posit Community. randomForestSRC questions: randomForestSRC documentation GitHub issues. filing bug, always include: Thank helping improve ggRandomForests!
code: # Minimum reproducible example library(ggRandomForests) library(randomForestSRC)  rf <- rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50) # ... the code that triggers the error ...  sessionInfo()  # paste this output into the issue

title: Random Forest Classification with ggRandomForests
text: Random forests (Breiman 2001) non-parametric ensemble method requires distributional functional assumptions covariates relate response. classification, tree votes class, forest reports vote fractions class probabilities. randomForestSRC package (Ishwaran Kogalur 2024) provides unified implementation survival, regression, classification forests. ggRandomForests extracts tidy data objects rfsrc fits renders ggplot2 (Wickham 2016), making straightforward explore forest constructed, variables matter, predicted probabilities depend individual predictors. vignette demonstrates complete random forest classification workflow Fisher’s iris data (Fisher 1936): Data exploration — EDA scatter panels, two variable pairs separate three species Growing forest — fitting RF, checking OOB error convergence Variable selection — VIMP minimal depth via max.subtree() SHAP analysis — per-observation, per-class additive explanations via gg_shap() Dependence plots — variable dependence partial dependence via gg_variable() gg_partial_rfsrc() Classification performance — ROC curves AUC via gg_roc()
code: library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(randomForestSRC)  if (requireNamespace("ggRandomForests", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(ggRandomForests) } else if (requireNamespace("pkgload", quietly = TRUE)) {   pkgload::load_all(export_all = FALSE, helpers = FALSE, attach_testthat = FALSE) } else {   stop("Install ggRandomForests (or pkgload for dev builds) to render this vignette.") }  theme_set(theme_bw())

title: Random Forest Classification with ggRandomForests
text: Fisher’s iris data (Fisher 1936) measures sepal petal length width 150 flowers, 50 three species: setosa, versicolor, virginica. small data set, feature , flaw: every figure vignette renders second, structure well-understood enough strange behaviour stands . Petal measurements separate three species almost perfectly; sepal measurements .  Petal length vs. petal width, colored species.  Sepal length vs. sepal width, colored species. setosa sits corner petal space, clean two. versicolor virginica overlap plots, sepals petals. Keep overlap mind — interesting case everything follows, since setosa trivial forest get right.
code: data(iris) ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) +   geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) +   geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2")

title: Random Forest Classification with ggRandomForests
text: grow classification forest using four predictors. rfsrc() function detects classification family factor response. forest grew 200 trees. confusion matrix confirms EDA suggested: every setosa classified correctly, versicolor virginica trade three misclassifications way, overall OOB misclassification rate 4%.  OOB misclassification rate vs. number trees, overall per class. setosa error drops zero almost immediately. versicolor virginica settle around 6% , overall rate levels near 4% well 200 trees — forest still learning time stop growing .  OOB predicted class probabilities, faceted flower’s true species. panel one true species; point one flower’s predicted probability one three classes. setosa flowers pin probability 1 setosa 0 two — ambiguity . versicolor virginica panels show real spread: flowers still classified confidently, handful sit 0.2–0.6 range forest genuinely unsure two .
code: set.seed(42) rfsrc_iris <- rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris,                     ntree = 200, importance = TRUE, err.block = 5) rfsrc_iris #>                          Sample size: 150 #>            Frequency of class labels: setosa=50, versicolor=50, virginica=50 #>                      Number of trees: 200 #>            Forest terminal node size: 1 #>        Average no. of terminal nodes: 9.75 #> No. of variables tried at each split: 2 #>               Total no. of variables: 4 #>        Resampling used to grow trees: swor #>     Resample size used to grow trees: 95 #>                             Analysis: RF-C #>                               Family: class #>                       Splitting rule: gini *random* #>        Number of random split points: 10 #>                    (OOB) Brier score: 0.02253931 #>         (OOB) Normalized Brier score: 0.10142687 #>                            (OOB) AUC: 0.994 #>                       (OOB) Log-loss: 0.11724994 #>    (OOB) Requested performance error: 0.04, 0, 0.06, 0.06 #> #> Confusion matrix: #> #>             predicted #>   observed   setosa versicolor virginica class.error #>   setosa         50          0         0        0.00 #>   versicolor      0         47         3        0.06 #>   virginica       0          3        47        0.06 #> #>       (OOB) Misclassification rate: 0.04 #> #> Random-classifier baselines (uniform): #>    Brier: 0.22222222   Normalized Brier: 1   Log-loss: 1.09861229 plot(gg_error(rfsrc_iris)) plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_iris))

title: Random Forest Classification with ggRandomForests
text: VIMP, computed randomForestSRC, measures increase OOB prediction error variable’s values randomly permuted across --bag observations (Breiman 2001). classification forest, gg_vimp() returns one column per class alongside overall score, plot() facets automatically.  VIMP ranking, overall per class. Petal.Length Petal.Width dominate every facet, Sepal.Length Sepal.Width barely register — consistent EDA. Look closely virginica facet: Sepal.Length small negative VIMP (colored differently), meaning permuting slightly helped virginica predictions fit. kind disagreement across facets exactly per-class VIMP breakdown ; single overall score hidden . (distinct question permutation-vs-varPro comparison covered vignette("varpro", package = "ggRandomForests"), contrasts importance measured rather class.) Minimal depth (Ishwaran et al. 2010) ranks variables close root node first split, average. threshold 1.88, selecting 2 variables: Petal.Length, Petal.Width. VIMP minimal depth agree.
code: plot(gg_vimp(rfsrc_iris)) md_iris <- max.subtree(rfsrc_iris)

title: Random Forest Classification with ggRandomForests
text: VIMP ranks importance averaged whole forest, change question ask. SHAP : classification forest predicts one probability per class, gg_shap() explains exactly one probabilities time, chosen .class argument. Ask class 1 (setosa) get contributions setosa probability; switch class 3 (virginica) every contribution recomputed virginica instead. Nothing else method changes — players, game, different payout split. target virginica (.class = 3), harder two overlapping species. four predictors, kernelshap runs exact mode, explaining 150 flowers takes two seconds — need random-sample workaround regression vignette uses larger, higher- dimensional Boston housing data.  Mean absolute SHAP value per predictor, virginica probability. two variables top VIMP minimal depth, Petal.Length ahead Petal.Width. Three different mechanisms, one answer dominant variables — disagreement worth watching ranking, top.  SHAP beeswarm virginica probability: every dot one flower’s contribution one predictor. Petal.Width shows clean gradient: yellow (wide petal) dots sit positive side, pushing predicted virginica probability ; purple (narrow petal) dots sit negative side, pulling . exactly expect EDA scatter — virginica widest petals three species.  SHAP dependence Petal.Width, virginica probability. upward slope states relationship number instead color: Petal.Width increases, contribution virginica probability climbs clearly negative clearly positive, crossing zero around point versicolor virginica petals start overlap EDA plot.
code: set.seed(43) gg_shp <- gg_shap(rfsrc_iris, bg_n = 50, which.class = 3) plot(gg_shp, type = "importance") plot(gg_shp, type = "beeswarm") plot(gg_shp, type = "dependence", xvar = "Petal.Width")

title: Random Forest Classification with ggRandomForests
text: Mean absolute SHAP value per predictor, virginica probability. two variables top VIMP minimal depth, Petal.Length ahead Petal.Width. Three different mechanisms, one answer dominant variables — disagreement worth watching ranking, top.
code: plot(gg_shp, type = "importance")

Petal.Width shows clean gradient: yellow (wide petal) dots sit positive side, pushing predicted virginica probability ; purple (narrow petal) dots sit negative side, pulling . exactly expect EDA scatter — virginica widest petals three species.
code: plot(gg_shp, type = "beeswarm")

title: Random Forest Classification with ggRandomForests
text: SHAP dependence Petal.Width, virginica probability. upward slope states relationship number instead color: Petal.Width increases, contribution virginica probability climbs clearly negative clearly positive, crossing zero around point versicolor virginica petals start overlap EDA plot.
code: plot(gg_shp, type = "dependence", xvar = "Petal.Width")

title: Random Forest Classification with ggRandomForests
text: Variable dependence shows flower’s OOB predicted class probability plotted predictor. classification forest, gg_variable() returns one probability column per class plot() facets , way gg_vimp() .  Variable dependence Petal.Width, faceted predicted-class probability. Three curves, three stories: setosa’s probability collapses zero past petal width 0.6, versicolor peaks middle range falls sides, virginica rises steadily zero starting around 1.0 — three-panel pattern beeswarm dependence plots already showed virginica alone, now visible three classes . Partial dependence integrates effects covariates, giving risk-adjusted view predictor’s marginal effect one class’s predicted probability (Friedman 2001). Unlike gg_shap(), gg_partial_rfsrc() .class argument classification forests; defers randomForestSRC::partial.rfsrc()’s default, targets first factor level, setosa .  Partial dependence petal measurements, setosa probability. curves fall sharply flatten near zero — risk-adjusted version EDA already showed: past certain petal size, flower essentially never setosa. Getting kind curve versicolor virginica instead means going back gg_shap(): re-run .class = 2 3, shap_dependence() new object picks wherever .class left .
code: gg_v <- gg_variable(rfsrc_iris)  plot(gg_v, xvar = "Petal.Width") pd <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_iris, xvar.names = c("Petal.Length", "Petal.Width")) plot(pd)

text: ROC curves AUC equivalent regression vignette — specific classification, “well forest separate class rest” well-posed question. gg_roc() computes curve one class time, indexed way gg_shap()’s .class, just name which_outcome.  ROC curve virginica vs. rest. AUC 0.993 virginica confirms forest separates well, even given overlap versicolor. setosa’s ROC curve worth plotting — right angle, AUC 1, geometric version confusion matrix’s perfect row.
code: roc_virginica <- gg_roc(rfsrc_iris, which_outcome = 3) plot(roc_virginica) calc_auc(roc_virginica) #> [1] 0.9928

VIMP (gg_vimp()) minimal depth (max.subtree()) agreed: Petal.Length Petal.Width dominate, every class. gg_shap() gave top variables, added something VIMP : signed, additive, per-flower contribution one class’s probability, chosen .class. gg_variable() gg_partial_rfsrc() traced marginal risk-adjusted versions curves SHAP dependence plot already hinted . gg_roc() calc_auc() closed loop classification-specific question SHAP VIMP answer directly: well forest separate class rest. pattern regression vignette holds . gg_*() function returns tidy object; plotting separate step. title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: Random forests (Breiman 2001) non-parametric ensemble method requires distributional functional assumptions covariates relate response. method builds large collection de-correlated decision trees via bootstrap aggregation (bagging) random feature selection, averages predictions smooth noise single tree carries. randomForestSRC package (Ishwaran Kogalur 2024) provides unified implementation survival, regression, classification forests. ggRandomForests extracts tidy data objects rfsrc fits renders ggplot2 (Wickham 2016), making straightforward explore forest constructed, variables matter, response depends individual predictors. vignette demonstrates complete random forest regression workflow Boston Housing data set (Harrison Rubinfeld 1978; Belsley et al. 1980): Data exploration — EDA scatter panels, variable descriptions Growing forest — fitting RF, checking OOB error convergence Variable selection — VIMP minimal depth via max.subtree() Dependence plots — variable dependence partial dependence via gg_variable() gg_partial_rfsrc() Variable interactions — conditioning plots partial dependence surfaces
code: library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(randomForestSRC)  if (requireNamespace("ggRandomForests", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(ggRandomForests) } else if (requireNamespace("pkgload", quietly = TRUE)) {   pkgload::load_all(export_all = FALSE, helpers = FALSE, attach_testthat = FALSE) } else {   stop("Install ggRandomForests (or pkgload for dev builds) to render this vignette.") }  theme_set(theme_bw())

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: Boston Housing data (Harrison Rubinfeld 1978; Belsley et al. 1980) standard benchmark regression. contains data 506 census tracts Boston 1970 census. objective predict median value owner-occupied homes (medv, $1000s) 13 predictors covering crime, zoning, industry, environmental quality, housing characteristics. use copy MASS package (Venables Ripley 2002). plot predictor response (medv), coloring sole categorical variable (chas, whether tract borders Charles River). loess smooth highlights marginal trend.  EDA: predictor vs. median home value, colored Charles River indicator. Even simple view, two relationships stand : medv lstat (lower status %) medv rm (rooms per dwelling). Keep two mind — expect random forest rank important predictors, rest vignette comes back check.

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: VIMP varPro rank much variable matters, averaged whole forest. tell much variable mattered one specific tract’s prediction — requires different kind accounting. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) borrows idea cooperative game theory: treat 13 predictors players splitting payout, ask much one contributed tract’s predicted medv, averaged fairly every order players joined game. result signed contribution per predictor per tract, contributions add exactly: forest’s overall average prediction (baseline) plus sum one tract’s contributions equals tract’s actual predicted value. importance measure vignette makes promise. gg_shap() computes contributions calling kernelshap, needs one prediction per predictor per background draw, per tract explained. Scoring 506 tracts expensive, explain random sample 40 instead, plenty see patterns fraction cost. Averaging absolute contribution variable across 40 tracts gives ranking directly comparable VIMP.  Mean absolute SHAP value per predictor. lstat rm come top , VIMP minimal depth — three different mechanisms, one answer. agreement reassuring, ’s next two plots SHAP earns keep. beeswarm plot one dot per tract per variable: horizontal position SHAP contribution (negative pulls prediction , positive pushes ), color tract’s value variable, scaled low high within row pattern doesn’t get washed variables different scales.  SHAP beeswarm: every dot one tract’s contribution one predictor. lstat shows clean color gradient: yellow (high lower-status population) dots sit negative side, purple (low) dots positive side. rm runs way. track EDA scatterplot already hinted , now stated contribution individual prediction rather marginal trend. Plotting one variable’s SHAP contribution value closest SHAP gets partial dependence plot, tract tract instead averaged.  SHAP dependence lstat. downward slope matches sign correlation directly: tracts lower-status population get pulled baseline prediction, tracts less get pushed . next section asks question second way, partial dependence averaged whole forest instead tract tract, two views agree shape even though ’re built entirely different mechanics.
code: set.seed(42) shap_sample <- Boston[sample(nrow(Boston), 40), setdiff(colnames(Boston), "medv")] gg_shp <- gg_shap(rfsrc_Boston, newdata = shap_sample, bg_n = 50) plot(gg_shp, type = "importance") plot(gg_shp, type = "beeswarm") plot(gg_shp, type = "dependence", xvar = "lstat")

text: simplest coplot conditions categorical variable. examine medv vs. lstat, split Charles River status:  Variable dependence lstat, conditional Charles River. decreasing trend holds groups, slightly higher values along Charles River every lstat level. investigate lstat–rm interaction, bin rm six quantile groups using quantile_pts() facet:  Predicted medv vs. lstat, conditional rooms-per-dwelling groups. Median values decrease lstat within every rm group, intercept shifts upward rooms. Smaller homes low-lstat (high-status) neighborhoods still command high prices. complement view — medv vs. rm, conditional lstat groups — completes picture:  Predicted medv vs. rooms, conditional lower-status groups. rm effect strongest low-lstat tracts (bottom-left panels) nearly flat high-lstat tracts, confirming meaningful interaction.
code: gg_v$chas_label <- ifelse(gg_v$chas, "Borders Charles River",                           "Does not border")  plot(gg_v, xvar = "lstat", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = st_labs["medv"], x = st_labs["lstat"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   facet_wrap(~chas_label) rm_pts <- quantile_pts(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$rm, groups = 6, intervals = TRUE) gg_v$rm_grp <- cut(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$rm, breaks = rm_pts) levels(gg_v$rm_grp) <- paste("rm in", levels(gg_v$rm_grp))  plot(gg_v, xvar = "lstat", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = st_labs["medv"], x = st_labs["lstat"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set3") +   facet_wrap(~rm_grp) lstat_pts <- quantile_pts(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$lstat, groups = 6,                           intervals = TRUE) gg_v$lstat_grp <- cut(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$lstat, breaks = lstat_pts) levels(gg_v$lstat_grp) <- paste("lstat in", levels(gg_v$lstat_grp))  plot(gg_v, xvar = "rm", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = st_labs["medv"], x = st_labs["rm"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set3") +   facet_wrap(~lstat_grp)

title: Random Forest Regression with ggRandomForests
text: visualize joint partial dependence medv lstat rm, compute partial dependence grid: 10 values rm, evaluated 25 points along lstat.  Partial dependence surface: median home value function lstat rm. Fill colour predicted median value. surface confirms strong interaction: home values highest lstat low rm high (upper-left corner), dropping steeply along axes. non-planar curvature — particularly sharp step near rm = 7 — demonstrates kind complex, non-linear structure random forests capture naturally.
code: rm_grid <- quantile_pts(rfsrc_Boston$xvar$rm, groups = 10)  surface_list <- lapply(rm_grid, function(rm_val) {   newx <- rfsrc_Boston$xvar   newx$rm <- rm_val   pd_rm <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_Boston, xvar.names = "lstat", newx = newx)   df <- pd_rm$continuous   df$rm <- rm_val   df })  surface_df <- bind_rows(surface_list) ggplot(surface_df, aes(x = x, y = rm, fill = yhat)) +   geom_tile() +   scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Median Value\n($1000s)") +   labs(x = "Lower Status (%)", y = "Rooms per Dwelling") +   theme_bw()

Partial dependence gg_partial_rfsrc() gave risk-adjusted version curves: concave lstat, threshold-like rm. Conditioning plots partial dependence surface pulled lstat–rm interaction, room-size effect strongest high-status tracts. Notice pattern . gg_*() function returns tidy object (often data frame, sometimes small list data frames); plotting separate step. Use package’s plot() methods default figure want, reach ggplot2 directly .

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: Random forests (Breiman 2001) non-parametric ensemble method requires distributional assumptions relation covariates response. Random survival forests (RSF) (Ishwaran et al. 2008; Ishwaran Kogalur 2007) extend method right-censored, time--event data growing trees log-rank splitting rule aggregating Kaplan–Meier estimates within terminal nodes. forest returns three ensemble quantities subject. First, survival function Ŝ(t)\hat{S}(t): probability surviving past time tt, bounded 0 1. Second, cumulative hazard function (CHF): Ĥ(t)=−log⁡Ŝ(t)\hat{H}(t) = -\log \hat{S}(t), unbounded, monotone-increasing summary accumulated risk. Third, mortality: expected number events subject accumulate followed indefinitely covariate profile, computed summing CHF observed time grid. Mortality probability; unbounded relative-risk score. Read single number ranking patients low high risk, survival chance. randomForestSRC package (Ishwaran Kogalur 2024) provides unified implementation survival, regression, classification forests. ggRandomForests extracts tidy data objects rfsrc fits renders ggplot2 (Wickham 2016), can see survival forest built, predictors carry risk, survival shifts across one. vignette demonstrates complete random survival forest workflow primary biliary cirrhosis (PBC) data set (Fleming Harrington 1991): Data preparation exploration — cleaning, EDA, Kaplan–Meier curves Growing forest — fitting RSF, checking convergence OOB error Variable selection — VIMP minimal depth via max.subtree() Dependence plots — variable dependence partial dependence via gg_variable() gg_partial_rfsrc() Variable interactions — conditioning plots partial dependence surfaces
code: library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(randomForestSRC) library(survival)  if (requireNamespace("ggRandomForests", quietly = TRUE)) {   library(ggRandomForests) } else if (requireNamespace("pkgload", quietly = TRUE)) {   pkgload::load_all(export_all = FALSE, helpers = FALSE, attach_testthat = FALSE) } else {   stop("Install ggRandomForests (or pkgload for dev builds) to render this vignette.") }  theme_set(theme_bw())  # Plotting constants event_marks  <- c(1, 4) event_labels <- c("Censored", "Death") event_colors <- c("steelblue", "firebrick")

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: PBC study consists 424 patients referred Mayo Clinic 1974 1984, 312 randomized trial D-penicillamine (DPCA) versus placebo. data described Fleming Harrington (1991) Chapter 0.2, proportional hazards model developed Chapter 4.4. use copy bundled randomForestSRC. convert days years, recode treatment factor, coerce low-cardinality numeric columns (five fewer unique values, including binary 0/1 indicators) factors. avoid converting binary columns logical randomForestSRC::partial.rfsrc() handle logical predictors correctly survival forests. Good practice modeling: scan categorical variables stacked histograms follow-time, continuous variables scatter plots colored event status.  EDA categorical variables. Bar color indicates factor level; white = missing.  EDA continuous variables. Points colored death event; rug marks indicate missing values. Look patterns missingness (white bars, rug marks) extreme values fall outside biological range. Kaplan–Meier estimate marginal survival curve covariate adjustment. serves -covariate baseline: fit forest, comparing KM curves forest’s OOB predictions lets us judge much predictive signal covariates add beyond raw event rate. restrict 312 trial patients construct KM curves gg_survival.  KM survival estimates treatment group 95% confidence bands. curves largely overlap, consistent original finding DPCA offered clear survival benefit placebo (Fleming Harrington 1991).  KM cumulative hazard estimates treatment group. can also stratify continuous variables. reproduce bilirubin groupings Fleming Harrington (1991) Figure 4.4.2:  KM survival stratified bilirubin groups. Higher bilirubin strongly predicts worse survival — effect random forest rediscover without prior specification.
code: data("pbc", package = "randomForestSRC") pbc <- pbc |>   mutate(     years     = days / 365.25,     age       = age / 365.25,     treatment = factor(       ifelse(treatment == 1, "DPCA",              ifelse(treatment == 2, "Placebo", NA)),       levels = c("DPCA", "Placebo")     )   ) |>   select(-days)  # Low-cardinality numerics (including binary 0/1) to factor. # NOTE: do NOT convert to logical — partial.rfsrc() fails with logical # predictors in survival forests (randomForestSRC <= 3.5.1). # Exclude the response columns (status, years) from conversion. resp_cols <- c("status", "years") for (nm in setdiff(names(pbc), resp_cols)) {   v <- pbc[[nm]]   if (is.numeric(v) && !is.factor(v) && length(unique(v[!is.na(v)])) <= 5) {     pbc[[nm]] <- factor(v)   } } # Human-readable labels for plot axes st_labs <- c(   status       = "Death Event",   treatment    = "Treatment",   age          = "Age (years)",   sex          = "Female",   ascites      = "Ascites",   hepato       = "Hepatomegaly",   spiders      = "Spiders",   edema        = "Edema (0, 0.5, 1)",   bili         = "Serum Bilirubin (mg/dl)",   chol         = "Serum Cholesterol (mg/dl)",   albumin      = "Albumin (gm/dl)",   copper       = "Urine Copper (ug/day)",   alk.phos     = "Alkaline Phosphatase (U/liter)",   ast          = "SGOT (U/ml)",   trig         = "Triglycerides (mg/dl)",   platelet     = "Platelets (per cubic ml/1000)",   prothrombin  = "Prothrombin Time (sec)",   stage        = "Histologic Stage",   years        = "Follow-up Time (years)" ) cls <- sapply(pbc, class) cnt_idx <- which(cls %in% c("numeric", "integer")) fct_idx <- setdiff(seq_along(pbc), cnt_idx) fct_idx <- union(fct_idx, which(names(pbc) == "years"))  dta_cat <- suppressWarnings(   pbc[, fct_idx] |>     pivot_longer(-years, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value",                  values_transform = list(value = as.character)) )  ggplot(dta_cat, aes(x = years, fill = value)) +   geom_histogram(color = "black", binwidth = 1) +   labs(y = "", x = st_labs["years"]) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdBu", na.value = "white") +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y", nrow = 2) +   theme(legend.position = "none") cnt_idx <- union(cnt_idx, which(names(pbc) == "status")) dta_cont <- pbc[, cnt_idx] |>   pivot_longer(c(-years, -status),                names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")  ggplot(dta_cont |> filter(!is.na(value)),        aes(x = years, y = value, color = factor(status), shape = factor(status))) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +   geom_rug(data = dta_cont |> filter(is.na(value)), color = "grey50") +   labs(y = "", x = st_labs["years"], color = "Death", shape = "Death") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks) +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +   theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.2)) pbc_trial <- pbc |> filter(!is.na(treatment)) pbc_test  <- pbc |> filter(is.na(treatment))  gg_km <- gg_survival(interval = "years", censor = "status",                      by = "treatment", data = pbc_trial,                      conf.int = 0.95)  plot(gg_km) +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)",        color = "Treatment", fill = "Treatment") +   theme(legend.position = c(0.2, 0.2)) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1.01)) plot(gg_km, type = "cum_haz") +   labs(y = "Cumulative Hazard", x = "Time (years)",        color = "Treatment", fill = "Treatment") +   theme(legend.position = c(0.2, 0.8)) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.02, 1.22)) pbc_bili <- pbc_trial |>   mutate(bili_grp = cut(bili, breaks = c(0, 0.8, 1.3, 3.4, 29)))  plot(gg_survival(interval = "years", censor = "status",                  by = "bili_grp", data = pbc_bili),      error = "none") +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)", color = "Bilirubin")

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: convert days years, recode treatment factor, coerce low-cardinality numeric columns (five fewer unique values, including binary 0/1 indicators) factors. avoid converting binary columns logical randomForestSRC::partial.rfsrc() handle logical predictors correctly survival forests.
code: pbc <- pbc |>   mutate(     years     = days / 365.25,     age       = age / 365.25,     treatment = factor(       ifelse(treatment == 1, "DPCA",              ifelse(treatment == 2, "Placebo", NA)),       levels = c("DPCA", "Placebo")     )   ) |>   select(-days)  # Low-cardinality numerics (including binary 0/1) to factor. # NOTE: do NOT convert to logical — partial.rfsrc() fails with logical # predictors in survival forests (randomForestSRC <= 3.5.1). # Exclude the response columns (status, years) from conversion. resp_cols <- c("status", "years") for (nm in setdiff(names(pbc), resp_cols)) {   v <- pbc[[nm]]   if (is.numeric(v) && !is.factor(v) && length(unique(v[!is.na(v)])) <= 5) {     pbc[[nm]] <- factor(v)   } } # Human-readable labels for plot axes st_labs <- c(   status       = "Death Event",   treatment    = "Treatment",   age          = "Age (years)",   sex          = "Female",   ascites      = "Ascites",   hepato       = "Hepatomegaly",   spiders      = "Spiders",   edema        = "Edema (0, 0.5, 1)",   bili         = "Serum Bilirubin (mg/dl)",   chol         = "Serum Cholesterol (mg/dl)",   albumin      = "Albumin (gm/dl)",   copper       = "Urine Copper (ug/day)",   alk.phos     = "Alkaline Phosphatase (U/liter)",   ast          = "SGOT (U/ml)",   trig         = "Triglycerides (mg/dl)",   platelet     = "Platelets (per cubic ml/1000)",   prothrombin  = "Prothrombin Time (sec)",   stage        = "Histologic Stage",   years        = "Follow-up Time (years)" )

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: Good practice modeling: scan categorical variables stacked histograms follow-time, continuous variables scatter plots colored event status.  EDA categorical variables. Bar color indicates factor level; white = missing.  EDA continuous variables. Points colored death event; rug marks indicate missing values. Look patterns missingness (white bars, rug marks) extreme values fall outside biological range.
code: cls <- sapply(pbc, class) cnt_idx <- which(cls %in% c("numeric", "integer")) fct_idx <- setdiff(seq_along(pbc), cnt_idx) fct_idx <- union(fct_idx, which(names(pbc) == "years"))  dta_cat <- suppressWarnings(   pbc[, fct_idx] |>     pivot_longer(-years, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value",                  values_transform = list(value = as.character)) )  ggplot(dta_cat, aes(x = years, fill = value)) +   geom_histogram(color = "black", binwidth = 1) +   labs(y = "", x = st_labs["years"]) +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "RdBu", na.value = "white") +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y", nrow = 2) +   theme(legend.position = "none") cnt_idx <- union(cnt_idx, which(names(pbc) == "status")) dta_cont <- pbc[, cnt_idx] |>   pivot_longer(c(-years, -status),                names_to = "variable", values_to = "value")  ggplot(dta_cont |> filter(!is.na(value)),        aes(x = years, y = value, color = factor(status), shape = factor(status))) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +   geom_rug(data = dta_cont |> filter(is.na(value)), color = "grey50") +   labs(y = "", x = st_labs["years"], color = "Death", shape = "Death") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks) +   facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +   theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.2))

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: Kaplan–Meier estimate marginal survival curve covariate adjustment. serves -covariate baseline: fit forest, comparing KM curves forest’s OOB predictions lets us judge much predictive signal covariates add beyond raw event rate. restrict 312 trial patients construct KM curves gg_survival.  KM survival estimates treatment group 95% confidence bands. curves largely overlap, consistent original finding DPCA offered clear survival benefit placebo (Fleming Harrington 1991).  KM cumulative hazard estimates treatment group. can also stratify continuous variables. reproduce bilirubin groupings Fleming Harrington (1991) Figure 4.4.2:  KM survival stratified bilirubin groups. Higher bilirubin strongly predicts worse survival — effect random forest rediscover without prior specification.
code: pbc_trial <- pbc |> filter(!is.na(treatment)) pbc_test  <- pbc |> filter(is.na(treatment))  gg_km <- gg_survival(interval = "years", censor = "status",                      by = "treatment", data = pbc_trial,                      conf.int = 0.95)  plot(gg_km) +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)",        color = "Treatment", fill = "Treatment") +   theme(legend.position = c(0.2, 0.2)) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1.01)) plot(gg_km, type = "cum_haz") +   labs(y = "Cumulative Hazard", x = "Time (years)",        color = "Treatment", fill = "Treatment") +   theme(legend.position = c(0.2, 0.8)) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.02, 1.22)) pbc_bili <- pbc_trial |>   mutate(bili_grp = cut(bili, breaks = c(0, 0.8, 1.3, 3.4, 29)))  plot(gg_survival(interval = "years", censor = "status",                  by = "bili_grp", data = pbc_bili),      error = "none") +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)", color = "Bilirubin")

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: Several predictors PBC trial data contain missing values (cholesterol, copper, triglycerides, others). handle two-step approach: first impute using impute.rfsrc(), uses random forest proximity structure fill missing values, fit survival forest complete imputed data. keeps fitted forest object free imputation state, required partial.rfsrc() work correctly. forest grew 150 trees, splitting 5 randomly selected candidate variables node, stopping minimum terminal node size 15.  OOB prediction error vs. number trees. error stabilizes well 1000 trees, indicating forest large enough reliable predictions.  OOB predicted survival curves. Blue = censored, red = death events. curve one patient’s OOB ensemble survival function Ŝ(t)\hat{S}(t), extended last follow-time. forest never saw patient building prediction, genuine --sample estimates. Red (death event) curves generally fall faster reach lower survival probabilities, confirming forest separates risk groups. Comparing spread marginal KM curves previous section shows much covariates tighten risk stratification.  Median predicted survival treatment group 95% confidence bands. non-trial patients (pbc_test) substantial missing data. predict.rfsrc() handles transparently prediction time via na.action = "na.impute" — distinct training-time imputation affect fitted forest object.  Predicted survival non-trial patients (test set). training curves use OOB estimates, plots represent --sample predictions directly comparable.
code: # Step 1: impute missing values via random forest proximity pbc_imputed <- impute.rfsrc(Surv(years, status) ~ .,                              data    = pbc_trial,                              ntree   = 100,                              nimpute = 2)  # Step 2: grow the survival forest on the complete imputed data rfsrc_pbc <- rfsrc(Surv(years, status) ~ .,                    data      = pbc_imputed,                    ntree     = 150,                    nsplit    = 10,                    tree.err  = TRUE,                    importance = TRUE) plot(gg_error(rfsrc_pbc)) gg_rsf <- plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc), alpha = 0.2) +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01)) gg_rsf plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, by = "treatment")) +   theme(legend.position = c(0.2, 0.2)) +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01)) rfsrc_pbc_test <- predict(rfsrc_pbc, newdata = pbc_test,                           na.action = "na.impute",                           importance = TRUE)  plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc_test), alpha = 0.2) +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   labs(y = "Survival Probability", x = "Time (years)") +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01))

text: Random forest uses available predictors. understand matter , examine variable importance (VIMP) minimal depth. VIMP measures increase prediction error variable’s values randomly permuted across --bag sample. survival forests prediction error Harrell C-statistic complement (1 − C), permuting important variable hurts C yields large positive VIMP. negative VIMP means variable adding noise: forest predict better without . See randomForestSRC documentation details VIMP calculation censored outcomes (Ishwaran et al. 2008).  Variable importance ranking. Blue = positive VIMP, red = negative. Bilirubin ranks highest, followed copper, prothrombin time, albumin, age — closely matching variables selected Fleming Harrington (1991) proportional hazards model. Variables partition large portions population early considered important. max.subtree() function computes minimal depth variable. threshold 5.94, selecting 8 variables: ascites, edema, bili, chol, albumin, copper, sgot, prothrombin. selection methods agree key predictors: bili, albumin, copper, prothrombin, age. add edema (selected Fleming Harrington (1991) model) remainder analysis.

code: gg_v <- gg_variable(rfsrc_pbc, time = c(1, 3),                     time.labels = c("1 Year", "3 Years"))  plot(gg_v, xvar = "bili", alpha = 0.4) +   labs(y = "Survival", x = st_labs["bili"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01)) plot(gg_v, xvar = xvar[-1], panel = TRUE, alpha = 0.4) +   labs(y = "Survival") +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.05, 1.05)) plot(gg_v, xvar = xvar_cat, alpha = 0.4) +   labs(y = "Survival") +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.02)) # partial.rfsrc() requires times that match the model's time.interest grid; # gg_partial_rfsrc() snaps the requested values to the nearest observed times. ti   <- rfsrc_pbc$time.interest t1yr <- ti[which.min(abs(ti - 1))] t3yr <- ti[which.min(abs(ti - 3))]  pd <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, xvar.names = xvar,                        partial.time = c(t1yr, t3yr))  # Quick S3 plot — survival forests produce time-series curves per predictor value plot(pd) ggplot(pd$continuous, aes(x = x, y = yhat,                           color = factor(round(time, 2)),                           group = factor(time))) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   facet_wrap(~name, scales = "free_x") +   labs(y = "Predicted Survival", x = "", color = "Year") +   scale_color_manual(values = setNames(c("steelblue", "firebrick"),                                        as.character(round(c(t1yr, t3yr), 2)))) +   theme_bw() gg_v1 <- gg_variable(rfsrc_pbc, time = 1) gg_v1$edema <- paste("edema =", gg_v1$edema)  plot(gg_v1, xvar = "bili", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = "Survival at 1 Year", x = st_labs["bili"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   facet_grid(~edema) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01)) albumin_cts <- quantile_pts(gg_v1$albumin, groups = 6, intervals = TRUE) gg_v1$albumin_grp <- cut(gg_v1$albumin, breaks = albumin_cts) levels(gg_v1$albumin_grp) <- paste("albumin =",                                     levels(gg_v1$albumin_grp))  plot(gg_v1, xvar = "bili", alpha = 0.5) +   labs(y = "Survival at 1 Year", x = st_labs["bili"]) +   theme(legend.position = "none") +   scale_color_manual(values = event_colors, labels = event_labels) +   scale_shape_manual(values = event_marks, labels = event_labels) +   facet_wrap(~albumin_grp) +   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.01, 1.01))

Brier score answers question. point event-time grid measures mean squared difference predicted survival probability Ŝ(t)\hat{S}(t) binary outcome (survived past tt ). right-censored subjects unknown true outcomes, calculation uses inverse-probability--censoring weighting (IPCW): subject’s squared error upweighted inverse probability uncensored time, estimate remains unbiased even heavy censoring (Graf et al. 1999). Two reference values help interpret curve. Brier score 0 perfect prediction. score around 0.25 uninformative ceiling: model assigns every subject survival probability 0.5, regardless covariates time horizon, scores near 0.25 construction. forest beats 0.25 better floor; one exceeds made worse covariates, sign overfitting poorly specified model. gg_brier() wraps randomForestSRC::get.brier.survival() returns tidy data frame ready plot.  Time-resolved Brier score PBC survival forest. Read curve left right. Early sits near zero — almost everyone still alive, predicting survival easy. climbs peak around median event time, outcome genuinely uncertain, falls -risk pool shrinks remaining predictions get easier. Setting envelope = TRUE adds 15–85% ribbon around line, showing spread individual subjects’ Brier contributions time. narrow ribbon means forest assigning similar risks across subjects; wide one means predicted risks pulling apart.  Brier score 15–85% per-subject envelope. running CRPS (continuous ranked probability score) Brier score integrated time divided elapsed time — time-average curve . collapses whole trajectory one running number, handy want single calibration figure rather curve read. Like Brier score, CRPS near 0 good value near 0.25 uninformative ceiling (constant-predictor reference ). final (integrated) value right edge plot one often reported.  Running CRPS PBC survival forest. integrated CRPS — scalar summary overall calibration — stored attribute can retrieved :
code: # Create grid of albumin values alb_grid <- quantile_pts(pbc_trial$albumin, groups = 8)  # For each albumin value, compute partial dependence on bili at ~1 year surface_list <- lapply(alb_grid, function(alb_val) {   newx <- pbc_trial[, rfsrc_pbc$xvar.names, drop = FALSE]   newx$albumin <- alb_val   pd_alb <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, xvar.names = "bili",                               newx = newx, partial.time = t1yr)   df <- pd_alb$continuous   df$albumin <- alb_val   df })  surface_df <- bind_rows(surface_list) if (!exists("surface_df")) {   message("surface_df not available --- skipping surface (see surface-data chunk error above).") } else {   ggplot(surface_df, aes(x = x, y = albumin, fill = yhat)) +     geom_tile() +     scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Survival") +     labs(x = "Bilirubin", y = "Albumin") +     theme_bw() } gg_bs <- gg_brier(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_bs) plot(gg_bs, envelope = TRUE) plot(gg_bs, type = "crps") attr(gg_bs, "crps_integrated") #> [1] 1.419147

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: VIMP minimal depth tell us predictors matter, say nothing well forest’s survival predictions calibrated. Brier score answers question. point event-time grid measures mean squared difference predicted survival probability Ŝ(t)\hat{S}(t) binary outcome (survived past tt ). right-censored subjects unknown true outcomes, calculation uses inverse-probability--censoring weighting (IPCW): subject’s squared error upweighted inverse probability uncensored time, estimate remains unbiased even heavy censoring (Graf et al. 1999). Two reference values help interpret curve. Brier score 0 perfect prediction. score around 0.25 uninformative ceiling: model assigns every subject survival probability 0.5, regardless covariates time horizon, scores near 0.25 construction. forest beats 0.25 better floor; one exceeds made worse covariates, sign overfitting poorly specified model. gg_brier() wraps randomForestSRC::get.brier.survival() returns tidy data frame ready plot.  Time-resolved Brier score PBC survival forest. Read curve left right. Early sits near zero — almost everyone still alive, predicting survival easy. climbs peak around median event time, outcome genuinely uncertain, falls -risk pool shrinks remaining predictions get easier. Setting envelope = TRUE adds 15–85% ribbon around line, showing spread individual subjects’ Brier contributions time. narrow ribbon means forest assigning similar risks across subjects; wide one means predicted risks pulling apart.  Brier score 15–85% per-subject envelope. running CRPS (continuous ranked probability score) Brier score integrated time divided elapsed time — time-average curve . collapses whole trajectory one running number, handy want single calibration figure rather curve read. Like Brier score, CRPS near 0 good value near 0.25 uninformative ceiling (constant-predictor reference ). final (integrated) value right edge plot one often reported.  Running CRPS PBC survival forest. integrated CRPS — scalar summary overall calibration — stored attribute can retrieved :

title: Random Forest Survival Analysis with ggRandomForests
text: walked full random survival forest analysis randomForestSRC ggRandomForests, results hang together: gg_survival() drew Kaplan–Meier curves showed treatment effect PBC trial, conclusion reached Fleming Harrington (1991). gg_error() showed OOB error settling quickly trees added. VIMP (gg_vimp()) minimal depth (max.subtree()) landed five predictors (bilirubin, albumin, copper, prothrombin, age), also proportional hazards model landed. gg_variable() exposed non-proportional hazards bilirubin copper, gap time horizons widens. Partial dependence gg_partial_rfsrc() gave risk-adjusted version curves backed log-transforms used parametric model. Conditioning plots partial dependence surface drew bilirubin–albumin interaction. gg_brier() measured accurate predictions actually , across time single CRPS summary. Notice pattern. gg_*() function returns tidy object (often data frame, sometimes small list data frames); plotting comes . Lean package’s plot() methods default figure works, drop ggplot2 directly .

title: Variable selection without an outcome: unsupervised varPro
text: three views one workflow, three unrelated plots. Start gg_udependent() see structure, rank gg_beta_uvarpro(), let gg_sdependent() draw signal-versus-noise line. derive one uvarpro() fit, two one get.beta.entropy() matrix, computing matrix () passing keeps whole sequence cheap. supervised side varPro — VIMP, partial dependence, per-rule lasso refinement, local importance, anomaly scoring — see companion varPro vignette.

title: Exploring variable importance with varPro
text: Random-forest variable importance , two decades, mostly meant permutation importance: shuffle column, measure drop OOB accuracy, repeat. works, score computed artificially modified data never appeared training set, predictors correlated shuffled column implausible counterfactual, neutral baseline. Knockoff-style methods clean , introduce synthetic features rely explicit distributional assumptions predictors. varPro (Lu Ishwaran 2024) takes different path: one grounded entirely observed data. Think decision tree long chain “/” clauses. varPro harvests clauses rules, rule identifies specific region predictor space handful variables jointly constrain response. measure one variable’s contribution, compares local estimator (response summary restricted rule’s region) “released” estimator constraint tested variable lifted. comparison, averaged rules trees, variable’s importance z-score. Every observation used contrast real training data; nothing permuted, nothing synthesised. “release” metaphor right one. Imagine holding rope several points along length (rule constraints). Releasing grip one point (keeping others place) tells taut section . Variables go slack released carrying load; variables stay taut redundant given rest. Operationally, varPro’s guided splitting steers tree growth rules concentrate informative regions, subsequent rule harvesting step selects rules survived importance pre-filter. default, variables noise threshold dropped reporting (importance z pre-filtered), final ranking contains variables earned seat table (see varPro tools reference worked implementation details). Split-weights encode variable’s relative importance tree node, propagating signal release-rule contrasts partial-dependence curves. Cross-validation via cv.varpro() returns three views importance ranking: $imp (default, point-estimate ranking), $imp.conserve (one-SD rule: variables whose lower confidence bound positive), $imp.liberal (variable positive point estimate). conservative cut right default want hand short list collaborator; liberal cut better exploratory screening ’d rather miss nothing. classification, importance decomposes $unconditional score (averaged classes) per-class $conditional.z scores. variable separates one class others uninformative overall, unconditional z can near zero single conditional z large; conditional view catches . One-hot consolidation via get.orgvimp() get.topvars() maps per-level scores back original factor, downstream reporting stays familiar ground. core machinery feeds five supervised ggRandomForests wrappers. gg_varpro() summarises per-tree importance distribution. gg_beta_varpro() refines release-rule contrasts per-rule lasso. gg_partial_varpro() turns release machinery partial-dependence curves. gg_isopro() scores observations anomaly using isolation-forest variant. gg_ivarpro() computes per-observation local importance. separate set unsupervised wrappers — gg_udependent(), gg_beta_uvarpro(), gg_sdependent() — reads structure uvarpro() fit response ; get walk-companion uvarpro vignette. vignette walks five supervised wrappers three worked examples: regression problem (Boston housing), classification problem (iris, binary multi-class), survival problem (PBC). closing section one-page reference matrix mapping wrapper forest families supports.

title: Exploring variable importance with varPro
text: start Boston housing data: 506 census tracts, 13 numeric predictors, median home value response. varpro() builds importance machinery (release rules, per-tree scores, splitweight vector) . Every subsequent figure section reads single fit. formula interface intentional: varPro model-independent sense doesn’t assume linear parametric relationship predictors response, require name response guided splitting something optimise toward. first question varPro answers one permutation importance answers: variables matter, ranked. gg_varpro() extracts per-tree importance scores draws distribution horizontal boxplot, one row per variable, sorted median z-score, variables configurable cutoff (default 0.79) highlighted. Read spread, just position: tight box means every tree agrees variable.  narrow boxes near top variables varPro confident : every tree agrees matter. Wide boxes straddle cutoff line ones look twice; forest disagrees . disagreement isn’t noise suppress: can mean variable matters rule regions others, exactly kind structured heterogeneity model-independent methods built detect. Variables fall entirely cutoff still drawn , just highlighted. completeness fit work: varPro’s defaults weakest screened upstream, plot never shows candidates . ranking view tells variables matter. Partial dependence asks next question: response change variable, holding others fixed? gg_partial_varpro() wraps varPro::partialpro() returns tidy frame parametric, non-parametric, causal partial-dependence curves.  panel single predictor. three curves correspond three estimators varPro carries (parametric, non-parametric, causal); read sensitivity analysis. three agree, stable signal; causal curve diverges others, ’s hint variable’s observed relationship response may partly driven correlation predictors rule regions, direct effect. partialpro() operates within local neighborhoods defined release rules, curves reflect happens part predictor space forest actually visited, global extrapolation fitted model. strength (--distribution extrapolation) limit (sparse regions predictor space wider effective intervals, even plot doesn’t always show explicitly). gg_varpro()’s ranking built release-rule contrast. gg_beta_varpro() re-asks question variable variable inside rule: fits one-predictor lasso response released variable, restricted OOB observations rule’s region. fitted coefficient (call “local β”) captures variable’s linear effect within rule’s neighborhood, globally. Aggregating mean(|β|) across rules gives one number per variable: regression-coefficient-flavoured importance, VIMP score, global slope. distinction matters practice. variable strong nonlinear global relationship may locally small β values inside single rule (local-standardisation step within rule normalises scale), many rules fire , aggregated mean still large. Conversely, variable nearly linear global effect concentrate weight handful rules, -rule variability β low. beta.varpro() expensive (glmnet per rule), wrapper accepts pre-computed beta_fit can iterate selection, cutoff, class choice without re-fitting.  Compare gg_varpro() . Disagreement diagnostic signal: variable ranks high low one whose local linear effect inside many rules real even release contrast modest. wrappers far score variables response. varPro also unsupervised mode: varPro::uvarpro() grows forest predictor matrix alone, three wrappers read fit — gg_udependent() draws cross-variable dependency network, gg_beta_uvarpro() ranks variables entropy contribution, gg_sdependent() marks cut signal noise. outcome play, get short walk-companion uvarpro vignette. Variable importance one axis; observation outlierness another. gg_isopro() wraps varPro::isopro() (isolation-forest variant scores anomalous training row looks) renders result ranked elbow plus density scores. score [0, 1]; wrapper’s convention “higher = anomalous” (opposite varPro’s native polarity; wrapper flips consistency).  elbow flags rows diverge bulk. Pair domain: anomalous feature space wrong, ’s often interesting cases live. regression context, high-scoring row one isolation forest isolated quickly feature combination rare; may may outlier response. Anomaly scoring residual analysis answer different questions, ’s worth . Note isopro() scores calibrated universal scale: score 0.7 one dataset comparable 0.7 another. matters relative ordering within dataset shape elbow: sharp kink small number observations cleaner signal gradual slope never levels . wrappers far aggregate across observations. gg_ivarpro() opposite: returns one value per (observation, variable) pair, capturing much variable v contributed predicting observation . aggregate view distribution local importances per variable; per-observation view horizontal bar one row’s local importances across variables. ivarpro() expensive call varPro, wrapper accepts pre-computed ivarpro_fit reuse across views.   distribution view tells variables drive predictions across observations. per-observation view answers question specific case, useful explaining one prediction back whoever asked. per-observation importance computed replaying release-rule contrasts restricted rules fire observation. Shapley value, shares local-attribution spirit: bar shows much variable’s release shifted local estimator specific row, averaged rules covered . Cases well-represented training data (dense rule coverage) sharper attribution cases sparse regions predictor space.
code: data("Boston", package = "MASS") set.seed(20260527L) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. v_boston <- if (is.null(.vp$v_boston)) {   varPro::varpro(medv ~ ., data = Boston, ntree = 50) } else {   .vp$v_boston } # A varpro fit is a large list; a bare `v_boston` would render every # component, including the full per-rule `$results` table. Show its # structure instead: one line per component. str(v_boston, max.level = 1) List of 13  $ rf              :List of 49   ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:3] "rfsrc" "grow" "regr"  $ split.weight    : NULL  $ split.weight.raw: list()  $ max.rules.tree  : num 150  $ max.tree        : num 50  $ results         :'data.frame':   6587 obs. of  5 variables:  $ xvar.org.names  : chr [1:13] "crim" "zn" "indus" "chas" ...  $ xvar.names      : chr [1:13] "crim" "zn" "indus" "chas" ...  $ yvar.names      : chr "medv"  $ x               :'data.frame':   506 obs. of  13 variables:   ..- attr(*, "hotencode")= logi FALSE   ..- attr(*, "xvar.names")= chr [1:13] "crim" "zn" "indus" "chas" ...  $ y               : num [1:506] 24 21.6 34.7 33.4 36.2 28.7 22.9 27.1 16.5 18.9 ...  $ y.org           : num [1:506] 24 21.6 34.7 33.4 36.2 28.7 22.9 27.1 16.5 18.9 ...  $ family          : chr "regr"  - attr(*, "class")= chr "varpro" plot(gg_varpro(v_boston)) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. gg_pd <- if (is.null(.vp$pd_boston)) {   gg_partial_varpro(object = v_boston) } else {   .vp$pd_boston } plot(gg_pd) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. b_boston <- if (is.null(.vp$b_boston)) {   varPro::beta.varpro(v_boston) } else {   .vp$b_boston } plot(gg_beta_varpro(v_boston, beta_fit = b_boston)) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. iso_boston <- if (is.null(.vp$iso_boston)) {   varPro::isopro(data = Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")],                  method = "rnd", sampsize = 256, ntree = 50) } else {   .vp$iso_boston } plot(gg_isopro(iso_boston)) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. iv_boston <- if (is.null(.vp$iv_boston)) {   varPro::ivarpro(v_boston) } else {   .vp$iv_boston } plot(gg_ivarpro(v_boston, ivarpro_fit = iv_boston)) plot(gg_ivarpro(v_boston, ivarpro_fit = iv_boston, which_obs = 1L))

text: Iris small data set (150 rows, four predictors, three response classes), ’s feature , flaw: every figure renders second, structure well-understood enough strange behaviour stands . also good stress-test conditional importance path: petal length petal width separate setosa everything else cleanly, versicolor/virginica boundary much softer. method reports unconditional importance lump cases together; conditional decomposition show asymmetry. Two fits: binary problem (drop setosa, positive class = virginica) full three-class problem. classification forest, gg_varpro() can split importance view one facet per class. Variables keep unconditional sort order rows line across facets; read along row see class variable informative . conditional importance uses $conditional.z varPro fit: per-class release-rule contrasts computed within rule regions unconditional score, local estimator replaced per-class probability. variable separates one class rest high $conditional.z class near-zero even negative value others. unconditional score $unconditional average, can mask class-specific effects, conditional = TRUE faceted view exists.  classification fit gg_partial_varpro() defaults probability scale (scale = "auto" resolves "prob"): predictor’s curve predicted probability target class — default last factor level (virginica), selectable target =. varPro::partialpro() works internally log-odds class; wrapper back-transforms observation probability averaging, curve mean predicted probability (probability mean log-odds). bounded [0,1][0, 1] scale parametric non-parametric curves shown — causal contrast log odds-ratio, level, share probability axis (use scale = "logodds" see ). scale = "odds" "logodds" give relationship odds log-odds scales. code: iris_binary <- iris[iris$Species != "setosa", ] iris_binary$Species <- droplevels(iris_binary$Species) set.seed(20260527L) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. v_iris_binary <- if (is.null(.vp$v_iris_binary)) {   varPro::varpro(Species ~ ., data = iris_binary, ntree = 50) } else {   .vp$v_iris_binary } set.seed(20260527L) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. v_iris_multi <- if (is.null(.vp$v_iris_multi)) {   varPro::varpro(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50) } else {   .vp$v_iris_multi } plot(gg_varpro(v_iris_multi, conditional = TRUE)) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. gg_pd_iris <- if (is.null(.vp$pd_iris_multi)) {   gg_partial_varpro(object = v_iris_multi) } else {   .vp$pd_iris_multi } plot(gg_pd_iris) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. b_iris_binary <- if (is.null(.vp$b_iris_binary)) {   varPro::beta.varpro(v_iris_binary) } else {   .vp$b_iris_binary } plot(gg_beta_varpro(v_iris_binary, beta_fit = b_iris_binary)) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. b_iris_multi <- if (is.null(.vp$b_iris_multi)) {   varPro::beta.varpro(v_iris_multi) } else {   .vp$b_iris_multi } plot(gg_beta_varpro(v_iris_multi, beta_fit = b_iris_multi))

text: Survival family varPro toolchain shows limits, explicit limits useful pretending don’t exist. forest-fitting family-agnostic wrappers work; lasso-refined individual-importance wrappers don’t, underlying varPro::beta.varpro() varPro::ivarpro() calls don’t yet extend right-censored outcomes. per-rule local lasso censored response requires local partial-likelihood Nelson-Aalen estimator place regression/classification local estimator; design work tracked yet landed. work core release-rule importance, partial dependence (survival probability S(τ)S(\tau) RMST release-rule engine, plus cumulative hazard via embedded $rf survival forest), anomaly scoring predictor matrix. many applied problems, three views cover questions actually want answer. PBC (primary biliary cirrhosis) dataset randomForestSRC 418 patients, seven predictors, Surv-encoded outcome days event (death transplant, status ∈ {0, 1, 2}). use small seven-variable subset vignette fits quickly. full analysis including time-dependent covariates, Lee et al. (2021) demonstrates boosted nonparametric hazard framework varPro’s survival path draws .  survival gg_partial_varpro() defaults survival probability (scale = "auto" resolves "surv"): curve S(τ∣x)S(\tau \mid x), predicted probability surviving past horizon τ\tau, computed partialpro() release-rule (UVT) engine regression classification fits — bounded [0,1][0, 1] read model’s time units. supply time, τ\tau defaults median follow-time fit. horizon derived data always model’s units mis-specified way hand-typed number can — units mismatch (days vs. years) classic survival partial-plot trap, data-driven default sidesteps . resolved τ\tau shown axis label reported message; pass time = tau choose another horizon. classification probability scale, causal contrast hidden (use scale = "rmst" "mortality" see ). survival scales explicit opt-ins: scale = "rmst" gives restricted mean survival time RMST(τ)(\tau) (also release-rule engine, median-follow-default τ\tau), scale = "mortality" keeps unbounded ensemble-mortality score (Ishwaran et al. 2008) — relative-risk index, survival probability.  isopro() sees predictor matrix, doesn’t care family. call section 3 works .  varPro::beta.varpro() errors survival fits current release (supports regr class). gg_ivarpro() survival similarly deferred pending design work per-rule risk-scaling story. tracked v3.1.0. call either survival fit ’ll get clear error message pointing deferred work, silent miscalculation. family-support matrix closing reference section records ; rest toolkit work survival (gg_varpro, gg_partial_varpro, gg_isopro ).
code: library(survival) data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC") pbc_small <- pbc[, c("days", "status", "age", "albumin", "bili",                      "edema", "platelet")] pbc_small <- na.omit(pbc_small) set.seed(20260527L) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. v_pbc <- if (is.null(.vp$v_pbc)) {   varPro::varpro(Surv(days, status) ~ ., data = pbc_small, ntree = 50) } else {   .vp$v_pbc } plot(gg_varpro(v_pbc)) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. gg_pd_pbc <- if (is.null(.vp$pd_pbc)) {   gg_partial_varpro(object = v_pbc) } else {   .vp$pd_pbc } plot(gg_pd_pbc) # Precomputed offline (see precompute_varpro.R); falls back to a live fit. iso_pbc <- if (is.null(.vp$iso_pbc)) {   varPro::isopro(data = pbc_small[, c("age", "albumin", "bili", "platelet")],                  method = "rnd", sampsize = 256, ntree = 50) } else {   .vp$iso_pbc } plot(gg_isopro(iso_pbc))

Three components carry three different counts: Boston fit : three coincide. fit uses completeness settings recommended , every predictor reachable ranking runs tail instead stopping screened top. fitting screens buys , counts boring. varPro’s defaults come apart, rest section . Even ranking shorter list, curve variable outside matter naming : partialpro() computes get.topvars() use default. Name xvar.names ranking ignored. second narrowing one surprises people. Guided tree-splitting (split.weight = TRUE, default) runs preliminary lasso decide variables worth splitting , variable never gets split never lands object$xvar.names. gone well partialpro() sees . binary iris fit, left varPro’s defaults something show, miniature: Four predictors go one never becomes candidate. p hundreds gap small. One sharp edge. partialpro() matches xvar.names object$xvar.names quietly drops whatever find. Ask twelve variables may get ten back, error, warning, nothing returned object say . misspelled name behaves way. matters, check: complete view, every predictor reachable reported ranking shows full tail, fit screens : split.weight = FALSE one lifts ceiling. guided splitting , every predictor stays candidate lands object$xvar.names, partialpro() can reach . safer sounds: partial curve variable already reachable barely moves, changing variables compete splits, local effect release rules estimate strong one. changes varPro’s importance ranking, weak variables now earn nonzero scores, read ranking screened fit screened ranking . sparse = FALSE smaller companion: deepens reported ranking tail shows, leaves object$xvar.names alone. Two ways fit, , two questions. Keep varPro’s defaults, screens , want parsimonious set: variables carry signal, ranked. Turn exploratory sweep, rather see every predictor filter judgment let screen decide, variable scores weakly can still one turns matter. nvar red herring either way; caps much gets reported, much competes.
