title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: Pre-release review v2.6.1 codebase — March 2026

title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: suite 15 test files covering major exported functions. high-level picture good — rfsrc + randomForest, regression + classification + survival paths, error handling — systematic gaps mean genuine bugs can pass undetected. Coverage summary

title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: Every test file uses one removed/deprecated calls: context("...") — deprecated testthat 3.0; harmless now break future release expect_is(x, "cls") — deprecated; use expect_s3_class(x, "cls") expect_equivalent(, b) — deprecated; use expect_equal(, b, ignore_attr = TRUE) test_shift.R: expect_that(x, is_identical_to(y)) — testthat 1.x, removed entirely Action: global find--replace across test files.

what: Gap 2 — Plots are tested structurally, not visually
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: Every plot.* test expect_s3_class(gg_plt, "ggplot"). ggplot object can created successfully even aesthetics map bare string literals (produces broken plot). means three broken aes() bugs described Part 2 pass test suite silently. Action: Adopt vdiffr::expect_doppelganger() least one snapshot per plot function. Minimum targets: regression scatter, classification jitter, survival step curve without CI ribbon.
code: # Example test_that("plot.gg_rfsrc survival CI ribbon snapshot", {   vdiffr::expect_doppelganger(     "rfsrc-surv-ci",     plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran, conf.int = 0.95))   ) })

what: Gap 3 — bootstrap_survival has zero direct tests
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: function tested via gg_rfsrc(..., conf.int = ...). unit tests verify output columns (value, lower, upper, median, mean), lower <= median <= upper holds, two-element level_set produces right column names. Action: Add test_bootstrap_survival.R calls ggRandomForests:::bootstrap_survival(gg_t, bs_samples, level_set) directly.

title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: test_gg_survival.R checks class runs 7 plot() type combinations, never verifies returned data frame actually expected columns (time, surv, lower, upper, n.risk, etc.). Action:
code: expect_true(all(c("time", "surv", "lower", "upper", "n.risk") %in%                   colnames(gg_dta)))

what: Gap 5 — Two expect_error calls test a non-existent function
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: test_gg_roc.R lines 59–60 102–103: gg_roc.rfrsrc exist, always errors "find function" — vacuously passes regardless whether input validation works correctly. Action: Fix typo gg_roc.rfsrc make error message explicit.
code: expect_error(gg_roc.rfrsrc(rf_iris))   # typo: "rfrsrc" not "rfsrc"

what: Gap 6 — Error message text is never asserted
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: Across test files, expect_error(fn(...)) used without asserting message. completely different error (e.g., NULL dereference) pass. Action: validated error path, pin message:
code: expect_error(gg_vimp(bad_obj), "This function only works for") expect_error(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_boston, by = c(1,2,3)), "correct dimension")

what: Gap 7 — Missing set.seed() and unbounded ntree
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: Many tests build forests without ntree (defaulting 500), without set.seed(). makes suite slow (CI times matter), non-reproducible debugging, fragile stochastic variation. Action: Every test builds forest open set.seed(42) pass ntree = 50 (ntree = 75 max).

what: Gap 8 — kaplan.R and nelson.R have no tests
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: helpers used gg_survival zero direct test coverage.

what: Gap 9 — nvar = 0 path for gg_vimp untested
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: gg_dta[1:nvar, ] nvar = 0 returns 0-row data frame (fine R), gg_dta[1:0, ] also works 1:0 c(1, 0) — two rows — actually silent bug. seq_len(nvar) fix applied elsewhere applied .

title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: Severity scale: 🔴 Bug (broken output) / 🟠 Logic error (usually silent) / 🟡 Code smell / ⚪ Style

what: 🔴 BUG — plot.gg_rfsrc.R: every aes() uses bare string literals
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: widespread breakage package. bare string aes() maps aesthetic constant — points end position. Affected lines (current file, recent edits): Additionally, geom_boxplot(ggplot2::aes(x = 1, y = colnames(gg_dta)[1])) line ~176 string-literal problem.
code: # Classification binary — line ~169 ggplot2::aes(   x = 1,   y = colnames(gg_dta)[1],          # ← string, not column ref   color = colnames(gg_dta)[2],   shape = colnames(gg_dta)[2] ) # Fix: ggplot2::aes(   x = 1,   y = .data[[colnames(gg_dta)[1]]],   color = .data[[colnames(gg_dta)[2]]],   shape = .data[[colnames(gg_dta)[2]]] ) # Classification multi-class — line ~195 ggplot2::aes(x = "variable", y = "value") ggplot2::aes(color = "y", shape = "y") # Fix: ggplot2::aes(x = .data$variable, y = .data$value) ggplot2::aes(color = .data$y, shape = .data$y) # Survival with CI, grouped — lines ~220–232 ggplot2::aes(x = "value", ymin = "lower", ymax = "upper", fill = "group") ggplot2::aes(x = "value", y = "median", color = "group") # Fix: ggplot2::aes(x = .data$value, ymin = .data$lower, ymax = .data$upper,              fill = .data$group) ggplot2::aes(x = .data$value, y = .data$median, color = .data$group) # Survival with CI, ungrouped — lines ~237–244 ggplot2::aes(x = "value", ymin = "lower", ymax = "upper") ggplot2::aes(x = "value", y = "median") # Fix: same pattern # Survival no CI — lines ~250–255 ggplot2::aes(x = "variable", y = "value", col = "event", by = "obs_id") # Fix: ggplot2::aes(x = .data$variable, y = .data$value,              col = .data$event, group = .data$obs_id) # Note: "by" is not a ggplot2 aes; use "group" for geom_step # Regression grouped — line ~268 ggplot2::aes(x = "group", y = "yhat") # Regression ungrouped — line ~271 ggplot2::aes(x = 1, y = "yhat") # Fix: ggplot2::aes(x = .data$group, y = .data$yhat) ggplot2::aes(x = 1, y = .data$yhat)

what: 🔴 BUG — plot.gg_roc.R: multi-class aes() uses bare strings
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
code: # line ~168–170 ggplot2::aes(   x = .data$fpr,   y = .data$sens,   linetype = "outcome",   # ← constant string, not column   col = "outcome" ) # Fix: ggplot2::aes(   x = .data$fpr,   y = .data$sens,   linetype = .data$outcome,   col = .data$outcome )

what: 🟠 LOGIC ERROR — bootstrap_survival: nonsensical negative indexing
title: ggRandomForests — Code Review & Testing Strategy
text: t(rng) n_time_points rows. negative index derived colnames(gg_dta), n_time_points + 2 (+3 group) columns. positions "obs_id" "event" always exceed n_time, negative index -op common case — intent wrong, code misleading, dataset two three time points silently drop real rows. Fix: gg_t feeds mn_bs already obs_id/event/group stripped.
code: # Current (lines ~475–479): dta <- data.frame(cbind(   time_interest,   t(rng)[-which(colnames(gg_dta) %in% c("obs_id", "event")), ],   mn[-which(colnames(gg_dta) %in% c("obs_id", "event"))] )) dta <- data.frame(cbind(time_interest, t(rng), mn))

what: 🟠 LOGIC ERROR — gg_rfsrc.rfsrc and .randomForest: is.null(df[, col]) does not detect missing columns

title: Visually Exploring Random Forests

title: Visually Exploring Random Forests
code: # CRAN (stable) install.packages("ggRandomForests")  # Development version from GitHub # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("ehrlinger/ggRandomForests")

title: Visually Exploring Random Forests
text: survival forests, see package vignette: variable importance varPro — partial dependence, importance z-scores, beta importance, individual/local importance, isolation forests — see dedicated vignette: unsupervised varPro tools — gg_udependent(), gg_beta_uvarpro(), gg_sdependent(), read structure uvarpro() fit outcome — short vignette:
code: library(randomForestSRC) library(ggRandomForests)  # 1. Fit a forest (regression) rf <- rfsrc(medv ~ ., data = MASS::Boston, importance = TRUE)  # 2. Check convergence: did the forest grow enough trees? plot(gg_error(rf))  # 3. Rank predictors by importance plot(gg_vimp(rf))  # 4. Marginal dependence for top variables gg_v <- gg_variable(rf) plot(gg_v, xvar = "lstat") plot(gg_v, xvar = rf$xvar.names, panel = TRUE, se = FALSE)  # 5. Partial dependence for a single predictor pv <- plot.variable(rf, xvar.names = "lstat", partial = TRUE, show.plots = FALSE) pd <- gg_partial(pv) plot(pd) vignette("ggRandomForests") vignette("varpro", package = "ggRandomForests") vignette("uvarpro", package = "ggRandomForests")

title: Visually Exploring Random Forests
text: gg_* function matching plot() S3 method hands back single plottable object: ggplot extend +, patchwork composite multi-panel methods. Every gg_* object also print() summary() methods: print() shows short header REPL rather dumping every row (use head() want rows), summary() gives diagnostics object can print keep.

title: Visually Exploring Random Forests
text: package built one decision: keep data step figure step apart. gg_* functions pull tidy data object forest; plot() methods turn object ggplot2 figure. Two things follow split. First, data object stands . carries everything plot needs, can save , inspect , come back later without keeping original forest — can large — memory. Second, never locked default figure. plot() method returns single plottable object (ggplot, patchwork composite multi-panel methods), can add layers, swap scales, apply theme; default want, can ignore entirely build figure tidy data .

title: Visually Exploring Random Forests

Recent highlights: v3.4.0 Unsupervised varPro wrappers (gg_beta_uvarpro(), gg_sdependent()) vignette; gg_partial_rfsrc() now handles factor predictors correctly. v3.3.0 varPro partial plots default interpretable scales — probability classification, survival S(τ) survival. v3.1.0 varPro integration: release-rule importance, partial dependence, local importance, anomaly scores, dependency graph.

title: Receiver Operator Characteristic calculator — calc_roc.rfsrc
code: ## Taken from the gg_roc example rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris)  gg_dta <- calc_roc(rfsrc_iris, rfsrc_iris$yvar,   which_outcome = 1, oob = TRUE ) gg_dta <- calc_roc(rfsrc_iris, rfsrc_iris$yvar,   which_outcome = 1, oob = FALSE )  rf_iris <- randomForest::randomForest(Species ~ ., data = iris) # randomForest stores the response in $y (rfsrc uses $yvar); pass the # original training factor so calc_roc has the class labels. gg_dta <- calc_roc(rf_iris, iris$Species,   which_outcome = 1 ) gg_dta <- calc_roc(rf_iris, iris$Species,   which_outcome = 2 )

what: ggRandomForests: Visually Exploring Random Forests — ggRandomForests-package
title: ggRandomForests: Visually Exploring Random Forests — ggRandomForests-package
text: ggRandomForests provides ggplot2 (Wickham 2009) diagnostic exploration figures random forests grown rfsrc (>= 3.4.0) randomForest. randomForestSRC gives unified treatment Breiman's (2001) random forests across data settings: regression classification forests response numeric categorical, survival competing-risk forests (Ishwaran et al. 2008) right-censored data.

title: ggRandomForests: Visually Exploring Random Forests — ggRandomForests-package
text: package built one decision: keep data step figure step apart. gg_* function pulls tidy data object forest; plot() method turns object figure. Two things follow. data object stands . carries everything plot needs, can save , inspect , come back later without keeping original forest – can large – memory. never locked default figure. plot() method returns single plottable object: ggplot extend +, patchwork composite multi-panel methods. Add layers, swap scales, apply theme – ignore default entirely build figure tidy data . Every gg_* object also carries print() summary() methods: print() shows short header rather dumping every row, summary() returns diagnostics object. Forest diagnostics gg_rfsrc: predicted versus observed values. gg_error: OOB error number trees. gg_vimp: variable importance ranking (Ishwaran et al. 2010). gg_variable: marginal variable dependence. gg_roc: ROC curves classification forests (see also calc_roc calc_auc). gg_survival: Kaplan-Meier / Nelson-Aalen estimates. gg_brier: time-resolved Brier score CRPS survival forests. Partial dependence gg_partial: tidies output randomForestSRC::plot.variable(partial = TRUE). gg_partial_rfsrc: computes partial dependence fitted forest directly, via partial.rfsrc. SHAP explanations gg_shap: SHAP values regression classification forests, wrapping kernelshap, shap_importance, shap_beeswarm shap_dependence figures. varPro rule-based variable selection gg_varpro: variable importance varpro fit. gg_partial_varpro (alias gg_partialpro): partial effects interpretable scale. gg_beta_varpro: per-region lasso coefficients. gg_isopro: isolation-forest outlier scores. gg_ivarpro: individual (per-observation) importance. Unsupervised varPro gg_udependent: variable dependency graph. gg_beta_uvarpro: entropy-based variable ranking. gg_sdependent: signal-variable detection. varPro required dependency (Imports), varPro families always available. kernelshap Suggests: gg_shap checks fails clear message installed.

High-dimensional variable selection survival data. J. Amer. Statist. Assoc. 105, 205-217. Ishwaran, H. (2007). Variable importance binary regression trees forests. Electronic J. Statist., 1, 519-537. what: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from an unsupervised varPro fit — gg_beta_uvarpro
title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from an unsupervised varPro fit — gg_beta_uvarpro
text: Tidy wrapper around varPro::get.beta.entropy() uvarpro object. gg_beta_varpro() refines supervised release-rule contrast, gg_beta_uvarpro() unsupervised analogue: uvarpro() builds entropy regions response, get.beta.entropy() fits cross-validated lasso within region ask strongly every variable explains released variable. Averaging absolute lasso coefficients per variable gives one number per variable: unsupervised, lasso-flavoured importance.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from an unsupervised varPro fit — gg_beta_uvarpro
code: gg_beta_uvarpro(object, ..., cutoff = NULL, beta_fit = NULL)

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from an unsupervised varPro fit — gg_beta_uvarpro
text: object uvarpro object varPro::uvarpro(). ... Forwarded varPro::get.beta.entropy() beta_fit = NULL (e.g. pre.filter, second.stage, use.cv). Ignored, warning, beta_fit supplied. cutoff Selection threshold beta_mean. NULL (default) uses mean(beta_mean); scalar sets explicitly. Variables cutoff flagged selected. beta_fit Optional pre-computed varPro::get.beta.entropy() matrix object. supplied, must numeric matrix column names (variables); ... ignored.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from an unsupervised varPro fit — gg_beta_uvarpro
text: gg_beta_uvarpro object (data.frame), one row per variable, -important first, columns: variable factor; levels reversed -important variable lands top coord_flip() (gg_vimp convention). beta_mean mean(|lasso beta|) released regions (colMeans(beta, na.rm = TRUE)). n_released number regions contributing non-NA coefficient variable. selected logical; beta_mean >= cutoff. provenance attribute records source, family ("unsupv"), cutoff, n_var, n_released_regions, precomputed.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from an unsupervised varPro fit — gg_beta_uvarpro
text: get.beta.entropy(o) returns (released-variable x variable) numeric matrix absolute lasso coefficients. column mean (na.rm = TRUE) per-variable importance reported , matching canonical sort(colMeans(beta, na.rm = TRUE), decreasing = TRUE) idiom varPro::uvarpro() help ("iowa housing - illustrates lasso importance"). get.beta.entropy() expensive (cross-validated glmnet per region), beta_fit argument accepts pre-computed matrix can iterate cutoff without re-fitting. pairing mirrors beta_fit argument gg_beta_varpro().

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from an unsupervised varPro fit — gg_beta_uvarpro

text: Tidy wrapper around varPro::beta.varpro() regression classification family. Aggregates per-rule lasso coefficient \(\hat{\beta}\) variable mean absolute value \(\mathrm{mean}(|\hat{\beta}|)\) flags variables scalar cutoff. Optional beta_fit argument lets callers compute expensive beta.varpro() step reuse result.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from a varPro fit — gg_beta_varpro
code: gg_beta_varpro(object, ..., cutoff = NULL, beta_fit = NULL, which_class = NULL)

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from a varPro fit — gg_beta_varpro
text: object varpro fit varPro::varpro() (regression classification family). ... Forwarded varPro::beta.varpro() beta_fit = NULL; ignored otherwise (warning). Documented forwardables: use.cv, use.1se, nfolds, maxit, thresh, max.rules.tree, max.tree. cutoff Selection threshold beta_mean. NULL (default) means mean(beta_mean) across released variables. Numeric scalar otherwise. beta_fit Optional pre-computed varPro::beta.varpro() result object. NULL (default) means wrapper runs beta.varpro() . supplied, must varpro-class object whose $results columns tree / branch / variable / n.oob / imp. which_class classification fit, name single response level subset . NULL (default) returns classes (binary fits resolve last factor level, positive-class convention used glm gg_roc). Ignored warning regression fits.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from a varPro fit — gg_beta_varpro
text: data.frame class c("gg_beta_varpro", "data.frame"). regression fit: one row per released variable, sorted beta_mean descending. classification fit: long-format extra class column, one row per (variable, class) pair; variable factor whose levels set mean(|sum--class-beta|) descending every facet / panel shares row order. which_class (binary default last-factor-level) collapses output single class.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from a varPro fit — gg_beta_varpro
text: Multivariate regression (regr+) survival families scope release tracked v3.1.0. unsupported-family path errors message pointing work.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from a varPro fit — gg_beta_varpro
text: Think varPro release-rule mechanism asking: "given region feature space forest carved , changes remove constraint one variable let observations leave?" standard importance answer (gg_varpro()) measures change z-scored contrast local estimators: synthetic data, permutation. beta.varpro() asks question different ruler: rule (tree-branch pair), fits one-predictor lasso regression response released variable's values, restricted OOB observations inside rule's region. wrapper aggregates per-rule coefficients one number per variable. key distinction gg_vimp(), measures Breiman-Cutler permutation importance perturbing variable's values watching OOB error climb, neither gg_varpro() gg_beta_varpro() touches data synthetically: contrasts real subsets defined forest's rules.

what: What imp actually is (pedantic, because the column name is misleading)
title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from a varPro fit — gg_beta_varpro
text: imp column beta.varpro()'s $results variable-importance score conventional sense. regularised regression coefficient. Specifically: Per rule, glmnet fits one-predictor lasso response released variable inside rule's OOB region. use.cv = TRUE selects \(\lambda\) 10-fold CV (default nfolds = 10); use.1se = TRUE (default) picks lambda.1se. use.cv = FALSE uses full \(\lambda\) path. imp absolute fitted coefficient \(|\hat{\beta}|\) chosen \(\lambda\). direction : varPro::beta.varpro() wraps every coefficient returns abs(), sign discarded upstream never reaches us. Read imp strength local association, direction. (signed local estimator, see gg_ivarpro(), sign survive.) Magnitude depends predictor's units (raw x, standardisation); predictor millimetres smaller \(|\hat{\beta}|\) predictor metres. Lasso shrinkage can drive \(\hat{\beta}\) exactly zero. zeros data, missingness, kept aggregation. Convergence failures land NA_real_ dropped. per-variable aggregate beta_mean, \(\mathrm{mean}(|\hat{\beta}|)\), across rules variable released. permutation importance, split-strength importance, directly comparable numeric axis gg_varpro()'s z-scores. Disagreement gg_varpro often diagnostic, bug. code form: imp_r glmnet coefficient \(\hat{\beta}\) fit y ~ x_v restricted rule r, \(\lambda\) chosen use.cv / use.1se.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from a varPro fit — gg_beta_varpro
text: One row per released variable. Columns: variable: predictor name. beta_mean: mean \(|\hat{\beta}|\) across variable's rules. n_rules: count rules contributing (zero-beta rules included; NA failures excluded). selected: beta_mean >= cutoff. Provenance attribute carries source, family, ntree, cutoff, cutoff_default, use.cv, n_rules_total, n_rules_nonzero, precomputed, xvar.names.

title: Per-variable lasso-beta importance from a varPro fit — gg_beta_varpro

what: Brier score and CRPS for survival forests — gg_brier
title: Brier score and CRPS for survival forests — gg_brier
text: Brier score asks familiar question probabilistic forecast: far predicted probability sit actually happened? survival forest forecast predicted survival probability given moment, "happened" whether subject still alive moment.  score computed every event time, get curve rather single number – lower better everywhere.  perfectly calibrated forest predicts 0 every subject died 1 every subject survived score 0; forest predicts 0.5 everyone scores roughly 0.25 regardless true outcome – "uninformative" ceiling.

Used plot.gg_brier(by_quartile = TRUE) draw envelope around overall curve. crps running CRPS (overall) time, normalised elapsed time. crps.q25, crps.q50, crps.q75, crps.q100 running CRPS within mortality-risk quartile. crps.lower, crps.upper running CRPS 15th / 85th per-subject Brier percentile, normalised elapsed time. integrated CRPS (single scalar matching get.brier.survival()$crps) attached attr(., "crps_integrated").

title: Brier score and CRPS for survival forests — gg_brier
text: function extracts time-resolved Brier score survival forest grown randomForestSRC, overall broken mortality-risk quartile (lowest-risk highest-risk subjects).  also returns continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) – Brier score integrated time divided elapsed time, running average summarises calibration point time axis. subjects right-censored, plain Brier score biased: censored subjects contribute outcome information yet still inflate denominator.  score uses inverse-probability--censoring weighting (IPCW), -weights uncensored observations compensate. censoring distribution estimated either Kaplan-Meier (cens.model = "km", default) separate censoring forest (cens.model = "rfsrc") censoring mechanism covariate-dependent. Internally, wraps get.brier.survival rebuilds quartile decomposition running CRPS returned brier.matx mort components, following approach internal plot.survival randomForestSRC.

title: Brier score and CRPS for survival forests — gg_brier
code: # \donttest{ library(survival)   # Surv() must be on the search path for rfsrc() data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC") rfsrc_pbc <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(days, status) ~ ., data = pbc, nsplit = 10 ) gg_dta <- gg_brier(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_dta)  plot(gg_dta, type = "crps")  plot(gg_dta, envelope = TRUE)   # overall line + 15-85% envelope   # Multi-model comparison: stack gg_brier outputs and plot with ggplot2. rf2 <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(days, status) ~ ., data = pbc, nsplit = 10, mtry = 4 ) compare_dta <- dplyr::bind_rows(   dplyr::mutate(gg_brier(rfsrc_pbc), model = "default"),   dplyr::mutate(gg_brier(rf2),       model = "mtry=4") ) ggplot2::ggplot(compare_dta,   ggplot2::aes(x = time, y = brier, colour = model)) +   ggplot2::geom_line()  # }

code: ## Examples from RFSRC package... ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## ------------- iris data ## You can build a randomForest rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris,   tree.err = TRUE, block.size = 1)  # Get a data.frame containing error rates gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_iris)  # Plot the gg_error object plot(gg_dta)   ## RandomForest example rf_iris <- randomForest::randomForest(Species ~ .,   data = iris,   tree.err = TRUE, ) gg_dta <- gg_error(rf_iris) plot(gg_dta)   gg_dta <- gg_error(rf_iris, training = TRUE) plot(gg_dta)  ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Regression example ## ------------------------------------------------------------  ## ------------- airq data rfsrc_airq <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Ozone ~ .,   data = airquality,   na.action = "na.impute", tree.err = TRUE, block.size = 1, )  # Get a data.frame containing error rates gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_airq)  # Plot the gg_error object plot(gg_dta)    ## ------------- Boston data if (requireNamespace("MASS", quietly = TRUE)) {   data(Boston, package = "MASS")   Boston$chas <- as.logical(Boston$chas)   rfsrc_boston <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(medv ~ .,     data = Boston,     forest = TRUE,     importance = TRUE,     tree.err = TRUE,     save.memory = TRUE   )    # Get a data.frame containing error rates   gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_boston)    # Plot the gg_error object   plot(gg_dta) }    ## ------------- mtcars data rfsrc_mtcars <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars,   tree.err = TRUE, block.size = 1)  # Get a data.frame containing error rates gg_dta<- gg_error(rfsrc_mtcars)  # Plot the gg_error object plot(gg_dta)    ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Survival example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## ------------- veteran data ## randomized trial of two treatment regimens for lung cancer data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") rfsrc_veteran <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ ., data = veteran,                        tree.err = TRUE, block.size = 1)  gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_veteran) plot(gg_dta)   ## ------------- pbc data # Load a cached randomForestSRC object # We need to create this dataset data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC",) #> Warning: data set ‘’ not found # For whatever reason, the age variable is in days... makes no sense to me for (ind in seq_len(dim(pbc)[2])) {  if (!is.factor(pbc[, ind])) {    if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {      if (sum(range(pbc[, ind], na.rm = TRUE) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }    }  } else {    if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {      if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }      if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(FALSE, TRUE)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }    }  }  if (!is.logical(pbc[, ind]) &      length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 5) {    pbc[, ind] <- factor(pbc[, ind])  } } #Convert age to years pbc$age <- pbc$age / 364.24  pbc$years <- pbc$days / 364.24 pbc <- pbc[, -which(colnames(pbc) == "days")] pbc$treatment <- as.numeric(pbc$treatment) pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 1)] <- "DPCA" pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 2)] <- "placebo" pbc$treatment <- factor(pbc$treatment) dta_train <- pbc[-which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ] # Create a test set from the remaining patients pbc_test <- pbc[which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ]  #======== # build the forest: rfsrc_pbc <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(years, status) ~ .,  dta_train,  nsplit = 10,  na.action = "na.impute",  tree.err = TRUE,  forest = TRUE,  importance = TRUE,  save.memory = TRUE )   gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_dta)

Lower means observation isolated quickly, anomalous. howbad Numeric [0, 1]; case.depth values pushed empirical CDF flipped higher means anomalous. score plot method draws default. provenance attribute records source = "varPro::isopro", observation count n, number trees ntree.

title: Tidy data from a varPro isolation-forest fit — gg_isopro
text: isolation forest (Liu, Ting Zhou 2008) random forest grown small subsamples data asked split observation lands terminal node. intuition geometric: typical observation sits dense middle feature cloud takes many splits isolate, unusual observation sits near edge gets cut . depth observation isolated proxy typical : shallow depth means anomalous, deep depth means ordinary. Average single observation's depth across many trees noise washes , leaving stable per-observation rank. isopro supports three flavours isolation forest, differ splits chosen: "rnd" original Liu/Ting/Zhou method: tree node picks variable random split point uniformly random variable's range. Fast, model, surprisingly effective. "unsupv" Unsupervised splitting randomForestSRC: splits chosen separate data along directions highest variance. structured "rnd"; sometimes accurate, especially anomalies follow coherent direction. "auto" auto-encoder formulation grows multivariate forest predicting feature others. expressive, slowest, best suited low-dimensional data. method universally best. varPro authors recommend trying least two comparing score distributions; plot method colours per-method curves automatically stack results.

title: Tidy data from a varPro isolation-forest fit — gg_isopro
text: fit gives back two parallel per-observation vectors: case.depth raw mean isolation depth (units "splits", lower = anomalous) howbad information [0, 1] scale, empirical CDF case.depth. fit two run way: varPro::isopro()'s howbad lower = anomalous, like depth came . flip . gg_isopro() returns 1 - object$howbad, column get higher = anomalous reads way score . transformation, fit's, means gg$howbad 1 - fit$howbad rather copy – worth knowing compare two side side. columns kept can plot either space raw depth hand diagnostics; howbad canonical score plot method uses default.

title: Tidy data from a varPro isolation-forest fit — gg_isopro
text: screening, inference. Reach want : flag observations may data-entry errors, --range measurements, distinct subpopulations fitting primary model; check whether held-cohort sits inside training distribution scoring model trained elsewhere; give analyst ranked list "look first" cases manual review; score held-cohort fresh batch incoming data fitted model compare test scores training distribution. score rank, probability outlier: two observations howbad = 0.92 unusual, "92\ likely anomalous". Pick cutoff looking elbow rises; plot.gg_isopro can annotate either score (threshold) top-percent (top_n_pct) .

title: Tidy data from a varPro isolation-forest fit — gg_isopro
text: Pass data.frame newdata extractor calls predict.isopro twice: quantiles = FALSE get raw mean case depth per row, quantiles = TRUE get per-row quantile depth training-data depth distribution. varPro's predict.isopro returns quantiles smaller anomalous, opposite polarity wrapper's howbad (higher anomalous). wrapper exposes conventions nothing hidden: case.depth carries varPro's native polarity, lower = anomalous. unmodified output predict(object, newdata, quantiles = FALSE). Use cross-reference raw varPro output. howbad flipped, wrapper-convention version. relationship howbad = 1 - predict(object, newdata, quantiles = TRUE). overlay training test scores one plot, bind two extractor calls method label column (column plot.gg_isopro uses colour rnd / unsupv / auto comparisons):
code: gg_train <- gg_isopro(fit) gg_test  <- gg_isopro(fit, newdata = test_df) gg_both  <- rbind(cbind(gg_train, method = "train"),                   cbind(gg_test,  method = "test")) class(gg_both) <- c("gg_isopro", "data.frame") plot(gg_both)

text: varPro framework builds importance release rules: given rule region, compares local estimator inside region estimator becomes constraint tested variable removed ("released"). contrast summed many rules trees get global z-score: quantity gg_varpro() shows. ivarpro() adds per-observation view mechanism. Concretely: ivarpro() walks forest's rules , (observation, variable) pair, computes scaled per-rule contribution predicting observation. Per-rule LOO removes observation rule scoring, contribution inflated observation helped define region. Per-region scaling (scale = "local", default) standardises contribution rule's local response standard deviation values comparable across rules different size. Aggregating per-rule scores one number per (obs, variable) pair gives local_imp cell. permutation, synthetic data: contrast always real subsets observed data, defined forest's rules. -synthetic-features property distinguishes gg_varpro() gg_vimp()'s Breiman-Cutler permutation importance.

Defaults training data stored rf_model$xvar. column names must match rf_model$xvar.names. partial.time Numeric vector desired time points survival forests (ignored regression/classification). NULL (default), three quartile points time.interest used. partial.type Character; type predicted value survival forests, passed partial.rfsrc. One "surv" (default), "chf", "mort". title: Partial dependence data from a varPro model — gg_partial_varpro
text: partial dependence curve answers question, "hold single variable grid values average everything else, model's prediction move?" question rfsrc partial dependence answers. varPro::partialpro adds two wrinkles worth understanding read curves. First, partialpro filters partial grid isolation forest (Unlimited Virtual Twins, UVT) unlikely combinations focal variable rest data downweighted. rfsrc version, contrast, averages full marginal grid regardless plausibility. covariate highly correlated others, two methods can disagree, partialpro's curve one restricted data manifold. Second, partialpro fits local polynomial model predicted values rather just plotting mean. gives three parallel curves per variable, stored yhat.par, yhat.nonpar, yhat.causal, plot method overlays can see whether smooth parametric story raw forest predictions telling thing. Interpretation y-axis depends outcome (per varPro::partialpro): response scale regression, log-odds target class classification, either ensemble mortality (default) RMST (original varpro call set rmst) survival.

text: Ishwaran H, Kogalur UB, Blackstone EH, Lauer MS (2008). Random survival forests. Annals Applied Statistics, 2(3), 841–860. doi:10.1214/08-AOAS169 . Ishwaran H, Blackstone EH (2025). title: Partial dependence data from a varPro model — gg_partial_varpro
code: set.seed(42) n_obs <- 30; n_pts <- 15 mock_data <- list(   age = list(     xvirtual    = seq(30, 80, length.out = n_pts),     xorg        = sample(seq(30, 80, by = 5), n_obs, replace = TRUE),     yhat.par    = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.nonpar = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.causal = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_pts), nrow = n_obs)   ),   sex = list(     xvirtual    = c(0, 1),     xorg        = sample(c(0, 1), n_obs, replace = TRUE),     yhat.par    = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.nonpar = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs),     yhat.causal = matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 2), nrow = n_obs)   ) ) result <- gg_partial_varpro(mock_data) head(result$continuous) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   variable parametric nonparametric  causal name  #>      <dbl>      <dbl>         <dbl>   <dbl> <chr> #> 1     30      -0.0695       -0.120   0.436  age   #> 2     33.6     0.149        -0.477   0.0438 age   #> 3     37.1    -0.237        -0.270   0.0531 age   #> 4     40.7    -0.0346       -0.0673 -0.0482 age   #> 5     44.3    -0.136         0.171   0.176  age   #> 6     47.9     0.0217        0.0159  0.119  age   head(result$categorical) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   parametric nonparametric causal variable name  #>        <dbl>         <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl> <chr> #> 1      0.850       -0.0338  0.751        0 sex   #> 2      1.76        -0.901  -0.829        0 sex   #> 3      0.846       -1.18    0.710        0 sex   #> 4     -0.545       -0.448   0.950        0 sex   #> 5      0.255        0.559  -0.579        0 sex   #> 6      0.299        0.0931 -1.23         0 sex    # \donttest{ ## The object-driven path: hand gg_partial_varpro() the varpro fit and let ## it call partialpro() for you.  This is where the reachability ceiling ## shows up (see Details). set.seed(42) vp <- varPro::varpro(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars, ntree = 50)  ## Three different sizes.  partialpro() can only reach the second. ncol(vp$x)                    # predictors in the data #> [1] 10 length(vp$xvar.names)         # what the fit reaches #> [1] 6 length(varPro::get.topvars(vp))   # the default when xvar.names is absent #> [1] 4  ## Say these came from an rfsrc VIMP ranking.  Check what the fit cannot ## reach before you spend the computation -- this is the habit worth having. wanted <- c("wt", "hp", "qsec", "vs") setdiff(wanted, vp$xvar.names) #> [1] "qsec" "vs"    ## Ask anyway and we warn, naming what partialpro() would have dropped ## in silence. pd <- gg_partial_varpro(object = vp, xvar.names = wanted) #> Warning: gg_partial_varpro: 2 of 4 requested 'xvar.names' are not in the varpro fit's reachable set and are silently dropped by varPro::partialpro(): qsec, vs. The fit reaches 6 of 10 predictors (object$xvar.names); varpro() screens in two stages, so a variable can be in the data and still be unreachable. Refit with varPro::varpro(..., split.weight = FALSE) to reach every predictor.  ## Refitting without the split-weight screen reaches every predictor. vp_all <- varPro::varpro(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars, ntree = 50,                          split.weight = FALSE) length(vp_all$xvar.names) #> [1] 10 setdiff(wanted, vp_all$xvar.names)   # empty; nothing to drop #> character(0) # }

title: Predicted response data object — gg_rfsrc.rfsrc
text: Every tree random forest makes prediction, forest's "ensemble" prediction average across trees.  --bag (OOB) variant averages trees include given observation bootstrap sample – built-cross-validation estimate requires held-test set.  gg_rfsrc pulls ensemble predictions fitted forest arranges plotting plot.gg_rfsrc.

title: Predicted response data object — gg_rfsrc.rfsrc
code: # S3 method for class 'rfsrc' gg_rfsrc(object, oob = TRUE, by, ...)

title: Predicted response data object — gg_rfsrc.rfsrc
text: object fitted rfsrc randomForest object. oob Logical; TRUE (default) return --bag predictions. Set FALSE use full -bag (training) predictions. Forced FALSE automatically predict.rfsrc objects, carry OOB estimates. Optional stratifying variable. Either character column name present training data, vector/factor length training set. supplied, group column added returned data bootstrap CI bands (survival) computed per group. Omit leave missing return unstratified result. ... Additional arguments controlling output specific forest families: surv_type Character; one "surv" (default), "chf", "mortality" survival forests. conf.int Numeric coverage probability (e.g. 0.95) request bootstrap pointwise confidence bands survival forests. Triggers wide-format output lower, upper, median, mean columns. bs.sample Integer; number bootstrap resamples conf.int set. Defaults number observations.

title: Predicted response data object — gg_rfsrc.rfsrc
text: gg_rfsrc object (classed data.frame) whose structure depends forest family: regression Columns yhat response name; optionally group column supplied. classification One column per class predicted probabilities; y column observed class labels; optionally group. survival (CI / grouping) Long-format columns variable (event time), value (survival probability), obs_id, event. survival (conf.int ) Wide-format pointwise bootstrap CI columns (lower, upper, median, mean) per time point; group column supplied. object carries class attributes forest family plot.gg_rfsrc dispatches correctly.

title: Predicted response data object — gg_rfsrc.rfsrc
text: structure returned data depends forest family.  regression forest get scatter OOB predicted values observed response.  classification get predicted class probabilities alongside observed class label.  survival forest get ensemble survival function (cumulative hazard, mortality, controlled surv_type) unique event time – one curve per observation – together trace range predicted risk cohort.  Pass conf.int add pointwise bootstrap confidence bands around mean survival curve, stratify predictor group. survival forests, use surv_type argument ("surv", "chf", "mortality") select predicted quantity. Bootstrap confidence bands requested passing conf.int (e.g. conf.int = 0.95); number resamples controlled bs.sample.

title: Predicted response data object — gg_rfsrc.rfsrc
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification example (small, runs on CRAN) ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- iris data set.seed(42) rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_iris) plot(gg_dta)   # \donttest{ ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Additional regression / survival examples are guarded with ## \donttest because the cumulative example time exceeds the ## 10-second CRAN budget. Run locally with `R CMD check --run-donttest` ## (or `devtools::check(run_dont_test = TRUE)`) to exercise them. ## ------------------------------------------------------------  ## -------- air quality data (regression) rfsrc_airq <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Ozone ~ ., data = airquality,                     na.action = "na.impute", ntree = 50) plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_airq))   ## -------- Boston data (rfsrc + randomForest) if (requireNamespace("MASS", quietly = TRUE)) {   data(Boston, package = "MASS")   Boston$chas <- as.logical(Boston$chas)   rfsrc_boston <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(medv ~ ., data = Boston, ntree = 50,                         forest = TRUE, importance = TRUE,                         tree.err = TRUE, save.memory = TRUE)   plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_boston))    rf_boston <- randomForest::randomForest(medv ~ ., data = Boston,                                           ntree = 50)   plot(gg_rfsrc(rf_boston)) }   ## -------- mtcars data rfsrc_mtcars <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars, ntree = 50) plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_mtcars))   ## -------- veteran data (survival; with CI and group-by) data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") rfsrc_veteran <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ ., data = veteran,                        ntree = 50) plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran))  plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran, conf.int = .95))  plot(gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran, by = "trt"))  # }

text: classifier hand class; hands predicted probability, pick threshold. Slide threshold 0 1 trade-catching positives crying wolf shifts whole way. ROC curve traces trade-. one class classification rfsrc randomForest forest, gg_roc walks every threshold records sensitivity (true positive rate) specificity (1 minus false positive rate).

title: ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve data from a classification forest. — gg_roc.rfsrc
text: object classification rfsrc randomForest object. forests family == "class" (rfsrc) type == "classification" (randomForest) supported. which_outcome Integer index character name class score. binary forests usually 1 2; multi-class forests, valid class index level name. which_outcome = "" 0 behaves differently engine: randomForest method Returns macro-averaged one-vs-rest ROC computed per-class probabilities. rfsrc method Warns falls back class 1. macro-average per-class faceting rfsrc path tracked separately issue #72. oob Logical; TRUE (default), build curve --bag predicted probabilities, otherwise full -bag predictions. randomForest, TRUE uses --bag vote probabilities object$votes; FALSE uses -bag predict(type = "prob"). per_class Logical; TRUE forest two classes, return one ROC curve per class, class scored others. result long-format data.frame class factor column named AUC vector attribute, ordered descending AUC. Binary forests treat per_class = TRUE -op. Honoured randomForest method . ... Extra arguments (currently unused).

title: ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve data from a classification forest. — gg_roc.rfsrc
text: gg_roc data.frame, one row per unique prediction threshold, columns: sens Sensitivity (true positive rate) threshold. spec Specificity (true negative rate) threshold. pct probability threshold used row. Pass calc_auc area curve.

title: ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve data from a classification forest. — gg_roc.rfsrc
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- iris data rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris)  # ROC for setosa gg_dta <- gg_roc(rfsrc_iris, which_outcome = 1) plot(gg_dta)   # ROC for versicolor gg_dta <- gg_roc(rfsrc_iris, which_outcome = 2) plot(gg_dta)   # ROC for virginica gg_dta <- gg_roc(rfsrc_iris, which_outcome = 3) plot(gg_dta)   ## -------- iris data rf_iris <- randomForest::randomForest(Species ~ ., data = iris)  # ROC for setosa gg_dta <- gg_roc(rf_iris, which_outcome = 1) plot(gg_dta)   # ROC for versicolor gg_dta <- gg_roc(rf_iris, which_outcome = 2) plot(gg_dta)   # ROC for virginica gg_dta <- gg_roc(rf_iris, which_outcome = 3) plot(gg_dta)

title: Nonparametric survival estimates. — gg_survival
text: object rfsrc method: fitted rfsrc survival forest.  default method: pass NULL (omit) supply interval, censor, data instead. interval Character; name time--event column data (default method ). censor Character; name event-indicator column data (1 = event, 0 = censored; default method ). Optional character; name grouping column stratified estimates.  rfsrc method, must column object$xvar. data data.frame containing survival data (default method ). type One "kaplan" (Kaplan-Meier, default) "nelson" (Nelson-Aalen cumulative hazard).  Default method . ... Additional arguments passed kaplan nelson.

title: Nonparametric survival estimates. — gg_survival

title: Nonparametric survival estimates. — gg_survival
text: Comparing forest's ensemble survival curve marginal Kaplan-Meier baseline quick sanity check: diverge forest found structure predictors carry; track closely predictors may add little.  gg_survival computes nonparametric baseline – Kaplan-Meier Nelson-Aalen estimate – can place canvas forest predictions gg_rfsrc. gg_survival S3 generic dispatches class first argument: rfsrc Extracts outcome columns fitted forest's $yvar slot (time column 1, event indicator column 2) delegates kaplan.  Use stratify predictor $xvar: get one Kaplan-Meier curve per group, ready compare forest's group-specific ensemble curves. default Accepts raw survival columns directly via interval, censor, data.  Delegates kaplan (default) nelson depending type.

text: named list class "gg_udependent" elements: $edges Data frame: variable_from, variable_to, weight (raw cross-importance value). $nodes Data frame: variable (factor, levels descending degree), degree (integer; -degree directed = TRUE, total degree directed = FALSE), selected (logical, TRUE sdependent's signal set). $graph igraph object. NULL dependencies detected. "provenance" attribute carries threshold, q.signal, directed, min.degree, xvar.names, n.

title: Variable dependency graph from a uvarpro model — gg_udependent
text: UVarPro (Zhou, Lu Ishwaran, 2026) extends varpro framework unsupervised setting: grow forest without response, use region-release contrasts varpro uses supervised importance ask, "variables explain structure data?" lasso-driven variant frames region-release contrast classification task (observation belong region release?) fits lasso logistic regression variables predictors. coefficient variable \(j\) model variable \(\)'s region-release contrast entry \([, j]\) matrix varPro::get.beta.entropy() returns. Read entry "much knowing \(j\) help separate \(\)'s region release". large \([, j]\) says \(j\) carries information structure varpro picked \(\). varPro::sdependent thresholds matrix user-chosen cut returns set "signal" variables: nodes high enough -degree worth keeping. pass threshold sdependent use matrix weight edges resulting graph. graph directed default \([, j]\) \([j, ]\) separate lasso coefficients need agree; setting directed = FALSE collapses pair taking larger two, appropriate want see two variables dependent, way dependency reads.

title: Variable dependency graph from a uvarpro model — gg_udependent
text: $edges one row per surviving edge raw weight [, j] (, undirected graphs, max two directions). $nodes one row per surviving variable degree (-degree directed, total degree undirected) selected flag membership sdependent signal set. $graph information packaged igraph object, weight, degree, selected attached plot.gg_udependent can render without recomputing anything.

title: Variable dependency graph from a uvarpro model — gg_udependent
text: screen wide unsupervised dataset small set variables UVarPro thinks carrying signal: nodes high degree, flagged selected = TRUE; spot clusters mutually dependent variables (hubs spokes around ) may measuring underlying construct; compare two datasets, two preprocessing pipelines, looking dependency graphs change. edge graph statistical dependency unsupervised decomposition data. causal arrow. high \([, j]\) says \(j\) predicts \(\)'s region membership, \(j\) causes \(\).

title: Variable dependency graph from a uvarpro model — gg_udependent

title: Marginal variable dependence data object. — gg_variable
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification (small, runs on CRAN) ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- iris data set.seed(42) rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50)  gg_dta <- gg_variable(rfsrc_iris) plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Sepal.Width") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'   # \donttest{ ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Additional classification / regression / survival examples are ## guarded with \donttest because the cumulative example time exceeds ## the 10-second CRAN budget. Run locally with `R CMD check ## --run-donttest` (or `devtools::check(run_dont_test = TRUE)`) to ## exercise them. ## ------------------------------------------------------------ plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Sepal.Length") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = rfsrc_iris$xvar.names, panel = TRUE)   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## regression ## ------------------------------------------------------------  ## -------- air quality data rfsrc_airq <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Ozone ~ ., data = airquality, ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_variable(rfsrc_airq)  # an ordinal variable gg_dta[, "Month"] <- factor(gg_dta[, "Month"])  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Wind") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Temp") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Solar.R") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = c("Solar.R", "Wind", "Temp", "Day"), panel = TRUE) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "Month", notch = TRUE) #> Warning: Ignoring unknown parameters: `notch` #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`?   ## -------- motor trend cars data rfsrc_mtcars <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars, ntree = 50)  gg_dta <- gg_variable(rfsrc_mtcars)  # mtcars$cyl is an ordinal variable gg_dta$cyl <- factor(gg_dta$cyl) gg_dta$am <- factor(gg_dta$am) gg_dta$vs <- factor(gg_dta$vs) gg_dta$gear <- factor(gg_dta$gear) gg_dta$carb <- factor(gg_dta$carb)  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "cyl")  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "disp") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "hp") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "wt") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = c("disp", "hp", "drat", "wt", "qsec"), panel = TRUE) #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta,   xvar = c("cyl", "vs", "am", "gear", "carb"), panel = TRUE,   notch = TRUE ) #> Warning: Ignoring unknown parameters: `notch` #> Warning: Ignoring unknown parameters: `notch` #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`? #> Notch went outside hinges #> ℹ Do you want `notch = FALSE`?   ## -------- Boston data if (requireNamespace("MASS", quietly = TRUE)) {   data(Boston, package = "MASS")   rf_boston <- randomForest::randomForest(medv ~ ., data = Boston)   gg_dta <- gg_variable(rf_boston)   plot(gg_dta)   plot(gg_dta, panel = TRUE) } #> Warning: Mismatched variable types... #>                   assuming these are all factor variables. #> Warning: Orientation is not uniquely specified when both the x and y aesthetics are #> continuous. Picking default orientation 'x'. #> Warning: Continuous x aesthetic #> ℹ did you forget `aes(group = ...)`? #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x' #> Warning: pseudoinverse used at -0.5 #> Warning: neighborhood radius 13 #> Warning: reciprocal condition number  0 #> Warning: There are other near singularities as well. 156.25 #> Warning: pseudoinverse used at -0.5 #> Warning: neighborhood radius 13 #> Warning: reciprocal condition number  0 #> Warning: There are other near singularities as well. 156.25 #> Warning: at  -0.005 #> Warning: radius  2.5e-05 #> Warning: all data on boundary of neighborhood. make span bigger #> Warning: pseudoinverse used at -0.005 #> Warning: neighborhood radius 0.005 #> Warning: reciprocal condition number  1 #> Warning: There are other near singularities as well. 1.01 #> Warning: zero-width neighborhood. make span bigger #> Warning: Failed to fit group -1. #> Caused by error in `predLoess()`: #> ! NA/NaN/Inf in foreign function call (arg 5)   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## survival examples ## ------------------------------------------------------------  ## -------- veteran data data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") rfsrc_veteran <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ ., veteran,   nsplit = 10,   ntree = 50 )  # get the 90-day survival time. gg_dta <- gg_variable(rfsrc_veteran, time = 90)  # Generate variable dependence plots for age and diagtime plot(gg_dta, xvar = "age") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  plot(gg_dta, xvar = "diagtime") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'   # Generate coplots plot(gg_dta, xvar = c("age", "diagtime"), panel = TRUE, se = FALSE) #> Warning: Ignoring unknown parameters: `se` #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'   # Compare survival at 30, 90, and 365 days simultaneously gg_dta <- gg_variable(rfsrc_veteran, time = c(30, 90, 365)) plot(gg_dta, xvar = "age") #> `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'  # }

Set FALSE keep raw importance scale, type = "raw" plot.gg_varpro needs. cutoff Numeric; z-score variable treated selected.  Default 0.79.  variable aggregate z cutoff flagged selected = TRUE $imp. faithful Logical; default FALSE.  TRUE, $imp.tree kept plot.gg_varpro can scatter per-tree points box. conditional Logical; default FALSE. title: Variable importance data from a varPro model — gg_varpro
text: named list class "gg_varpro" elements: $imp Summary data frame: variable (factor whose levels run least- -important median per-tree z, -important variable sits top plot coord_flip), z (aggregate z-score importance()), selected (logical, z > cutoff). $imp.tree NULL faithful = FALSE; otherwise ntree x p matrix per-tree importance values. $stats Per-variable summary: variable, median, q05, q15, q85, q95 (z-scale local.std = TRUE, raw FALSE), plus mean (raw importance mean, always stored). $conditional NULL conditional = FALSE; otherwise data frame columns variable, class, z (one row per variable x class combination). "provenance" attribute carries family, local.std, cutoff, faithful, conditional, xvar.names, n.

title: Variable importance data from a varPro model — gg_varpro
text: Permutation importance asks "happens OOB accuracy scramble variable?" works, leans artificial data (permuted column) answer can unstable variables correlated. varpro framework (Lu Ishwaran, 2024) replaces permutation release rules. forest grown guided splitting; subset trees varpro samples collection decision-rule branches; variable compares response inside rule's region response rule's constraint variable "released". size change, aggregated many rules trees, variable's importance. synthetic covariates, permutation: contrast two real subsets data. varpro builds importance rules sampled trees, every tree contributes importance value variable. per-tree scores summarise . local.std = TRUE (default) per-tree values standardised column standard deviation column mean equals aggregate z-score returned varPro::importance(); z-score canonical "variable ?" statistic, cutoff = 0.79 varpro's default selection threshold. classification forest, varpro also returns class-conditional z table: importance computed restricting attention rules relevant class. conditional = TRUE keeps table plot method can show variables matter class rather aggregate.

title: Variable importance data from a varPro model — gg_varpro
text: $imp one-row-per-variable summary: aggregate z varPro::importance(), plus selected flag z > cutoff. $stats holds box quantiles (5/15/50/85/95 percentiles, plus raw mean) computed per-tree matrix; boxplot draws. $imp.tree per-tree matrix , kept faithful = TRUE plot method can scatter individual tree values box. $conditional tidy class x variable z table, present conditional = TRUE family classification.

title: Variable importance data from a varPro model — gg_varpro
text: rank candidate variables importance pick working set varpro's z cutoff; see, via boxplot's spread per-tree points (faithful = TRUE), stable variable's importance across trees: high median wide box different story high median tight box; classification forest, ask variables drive class (conditional = TRUE) rather just variables drive model overall. z-score standardised ranking statistic, p-value probability. Two variables z "similarly important method", "equally likely true signal". data-driven cutoff rather 0.79 default, see varPro::cv.varpro.

title: Variable importance data from a varPro model — gg_varpro

title: Variable Importance (VIMP) data object — gg_vimp
text: gg_vimp() reports whatever importance forest stored; computes nothing .  Usually permutation (Breiman-Cutler) variable importance: forest permutes variable's observed values across --bag (OOB) cases, runs perturbed cases already-grown trees, measures much OOB prediction error climbs. perturbation synthetic (variable's link response broken purpose) large increase means variable carrying genuine signal; near-zero negative values mean added noise nothing . randomForest fit needs importance = TRUE give .  randomForest::randomForest() defaults importance = FALSE, fit stores IncNodePurity – node-impurity (RSS Gini) measure, permutation quantity comparable one.  importance forest kept, gg_vimp() reports, vimp column, .  Nothing marks difference plot.  gg_vimp(randomForest(y ~ ., data)) ranks node purity; pass importance = TRUE get permutation VIMP (%IncMSE), colnames(object$importance) tells one . randomForestSRC::rfsrc() trap: importance argument yields permutation VIMP. randomForest fit carries measures, gg_vimp() reports permutation one leaves node purity ranking – two run different scales mean different things, putting one ordering meaningless.  Read randomForest::importance(object) want .  classification fit names pair MeanDecreaseAccuracy MeanDecreaseGini, stores permutation column per class besides.  per-class columns permutation measures one scale, gg_vimp() keeps together, names set column, drops Gini one. gg_varpro() takes opposite route, comparing local estimators real observed data varPro's release rules, permutation synthetic features.  two approaches answer "variables matter?" opposite mechanisms, variable can rank differently , disagreement informative: often signals interaction structure non-monotone effects one mechanism surfaces obscures. survival forests, VIMP measured ensemble cumulative hazard function (CHF); error metric one minus concordance index (C-statistic).  Variables non-positive VIMP flagged positive column colored differently plot.gg_vimp.

title: Variable Importance (VIMP) data object — gg_vimp
text: Ishwaran H. (2007). Variable importance binary regression trees forests, Electronic J. Statist., 1:519-537.

title: Variable Importance (VIMP) data object — gg_vimp
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- iris data rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ .,   data = iris,   importance = TRUE ) gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_iris) plot(gg_dta)   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## regression example ## ------------------------------------------------------------  ## -------- air quality data rfsrc_airq <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Ozone ~ ., airquality,   importance = TRUE ) gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_airq) plot(gg_dta)    ## -------- Boston data if (requireNamespace("MASS", quietly = TRUE)) {   data(Boston, package = "MASS")   rfsrc_boston <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(medv ~ ., Boston,     importance = TRUE   )   gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_boston)   plot(gg_dta)    ## importance = TRUE for permutation VIMP; without it randomForest stores   ## only IncNodePurity, which is what you would be ranking (see Details).   rf_boston <- randomForest::randomForest(medv ~ ., Boston,     importance = TRUE   )   gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rf_boston)   plot(gg_dta) }    ## -------- mtcars data rfsrc_mtcars <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(mpg ~ .,   data = mtcars,   importance = TRUE ) gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_mtcars) plot(gg_dta)   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## survival example ## ------------------------------------------------------------  ## -------- veteran data data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") rfsrc_veteran <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ .,   data = veteran,   ntree = 100,   importance = TRUE )  gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_veteran) plot(gg_dta)   ## -------- pbc data # We need to create this dataset data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC", ) #> Warning: data set ‘’ not found # For whatever reason, the age variable is in days... # makes no sense to me for (ind in seq_len(dim(pbc)[2])) {   if (!is.factor(pbc[, ind])) {     if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {       if (sum(range(pbc[, ind], na.rm = TRUE) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }     }   } else {     if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {       if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }       if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(FALSE, TRUE)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }     }   }   if (!is.logical(pbc[, ind]) &     length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 5) {     pbc[, ind] <- factor(pbc[, ind])   } } # Convert age to years pbc$age <- pbc$age / 364.24  pbc$years <- pbc$days / 364.24 pbc <- pbc[, -which(colnames(pbc) == "days")] pbc$treatment <- as.numeric(pbc$treatment) pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 1)] <- "DPCA" pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 2)] <- "placebo" pbc$treatment <- factor(pbc$treatment) dta_train <- pbc[-which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ] # Create a test set from the remaining patients pbc_test <- pbc[which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ]  # ======== # build the forest: rfsrc_pbc <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(years, status) ~ .,   dta_train,   nsplit = 10,   na.action = "na.impute",   forest = TRUE,   importance = TRUE,   save.memory = TRUE )  gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_dta)   # Restrict to only the top 10. gg_dta <- gg_vimp(rfsrc_pbc, nvar = 10) plot(gg_dta)

title: Plot a gg_error object — plot.gg_error
code: ## Examples from RFSRC package... ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## ------------- iris data ## You can build a randomForest rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris,   forest = TRUE,   importance = TRUE,   tree.err = TRUE,   save.memory = TRUE)  # Get a data.frame containing error rates gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_iris)  # Plot the gg_error object plot(gg_dta)   ## RandomForest example rf_iris <- randomForest::randomForest(Species ~ .,   data = iris,   forest = TRUE,   importance = TRUE,   tree.err = TRUE,   save.memory = TRUE ) gg_dta <- gg_error(rf_iris) plot(gg_dta)   gg_dta <- gg_error(rf_iris, training = TRUE) plot(gg_dta)  ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Regression example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## ------------- airq data rfsrc_airq <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Ozone ~ .,   data = airquality,   na.action = "na.impute",   forest = TRUE,   importance = TRUE,   tree.err = TRUE,   save.memory = TRUE )  # Get a data.frame containing error rates gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_airq)  # Plot the gg_error object plot(gg_dta)    ## ------------- Boston data if (requireNamespace("MASS", quietly = TRUE)) {   data(Boston, package = "MASS")   Boston$chas <- as.logical(Boston$chas)   rfsrc_boston <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(medv ~ .,     data = Boston,     forest = TRUE,     importance = TRUE,     tree.err = TRUE,     save.memory = TRUE   )    # Get a data.frame containing error rates   gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_boston)    # Plot the gg_error object   plot(gg_dta) }   ## ------------- mtcars data rfsrc_mtcars <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars,   importance = TRUE,   save.memory = TRUE,   forest = TRUE,   tree.err = TRUE)  # Get a data.frame containing error rates gg_dta<- gg_error(rfsrc_mtcars)  # Plot the gg_error object plot(gg_dta)    ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Survival example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## ------------- veteran data ## randomized trial of two treatment regimens for lung cancer data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") rfsrc_veteran <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ ., data = veteran,                        tree.err = TRUE)  gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_veteran) plot(gg_dta)   ## ------------- pbc data # Load a cached randomForestSRC object # We need to create this dataset data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC",) #> Warning: data set ‘’ not found # For whatever reason, the age variable is in days... makes no sense to me for (ind in seq_len(dim(pbc)[2])) {  if (!is.factor(pbc[, ind])) {    if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {      if (sum(range(pbc[, ind], na.rm = TRUE) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }    }  } else {    if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {      if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }      if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(FALSE, TRUE)) == 2) {        pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])      }    }  }  if (!is.logical(pbc[, ind]) &      length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 5) {    pbc[, ind] <- factor(pbc[, ind])  } } #Convert age to years pbc$age <- pbc$age / 364.24  pbc$years <- pbc$days / 364.24 pbc <- pbc[, -which(colnames(pbc) == "days")] pbc$treatment <- as.numeric(pbc$treatment) pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 1)] <- "DPCA" pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 2)] <- "placebo" pbc$treatment <- factor(pbc$treatment) dta_train <- pbc[-which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ] # Create a test set from the remaining patients pbc_test <- pbc[which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ]  #======== # build the forest: rfsrc_pbc <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(years, status) ~ .,  dta_train,  nsplit = 10,  na.action = "na.impute",  tree.err = TRUE,  forest = TRUE,  importance = TRUE,  save.memory = TRUE )   gg_dta <- gg_error(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_dta)

title: Plot a gg_partial_rfsrc object — plot.gg_partial_rfsrc
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Regression forest -- one continuous curve per variable ## ------------------------------------------------------------ set.seed(42) airq <- na.omit(airquality) rfsrc_airq <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Ozone ~ ., data = airq, ntree = 50)  pd <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_airq, xvar.names = c("Wind", "Temp"),                        n_eval = 10) plot(pd)   # \donttest{ ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Survival forest -- one curve per requested time horizon, ## faceted by variable. Y-axis label tracks `partial.type`. ## ------------------------------------------------------------ # randomForestSRC's formula parser requires the unqualified Surv() symbol; # it Depends on `survival`, so Surv is on the search path once # randomForestSRC is loaded. data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") set.seed(42) rfsrc_v <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ .,                                   data = veteran, ntree = 50) ti  <- rfsrc_v$time.interest t30 <- ti[which.min(abs(ti - 30))] t90 <- ti[which.min(abs(ti - 90))]  # Default partial.type = "surv" -> y-axis "Predicted Survival" pd_s <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_v, xvar.names = "age",                          partial.time = c(t30, t90), n_eval = 8) plot(pd_s)   # partial.type = "chf" -> y-axis "Predicted CHF" pd_c <- gg_partial_rfsrc(rfsrc_v, xvar.names = "age",                          partial.time = c(t30, t90),                          partial.type = "chf", n_eval = 8) plot(pd_c)  # }

what: What the causal curve is, and when to use it
title: Plot a gg_partial_varpro object — plot.gg_partial_varpro
text: causal baseline-subtracted local effect – varPro's virtual- ("digital-") twins estimator (Ishwaran & Blackstone, 2025).  shows prediction shifts focal variable moves away reference grid point, covariates held -manifold (UVT-plausible) values; contrast (starts 0), level.  Use want local effect (change--baseline) rather absolute predicted level, cross-check parametric nonparametric curves.  varpro's local estimator within fitted model, structural causal claim data-generating process.  contrast share probability/odds axis absolute curves, shown additive scales ("logodds", "mortality", "rmst").

title: Plot a gg_partial_varpro object — plot.gg_partial_varpro
text: y-axis restricted mean survival time horizon \(\tau\), \(\mathrm{RMST}(\tau)=\int_0^\tau S(t)\,dt\): expected event-free time first \(\tau\) time-units, area survival curve \(\tau\). Read model's time units, bounded \(0 \le \mathrm{RMST}(\tau) \le \tau\). Two things follow. First, \(\tau\) must given fit's time units; \(\tau\) past largest event time just truncates full restricted mean stops changing. Second, higher better – time event-free – opposite ensemble-mortality scale. continuous variable's curve sloping means higher values covariate buy restricted-mean event-free time within \(\tau\) (covariates held UVT-plausible average); flat curve means covariate move . Unlike ensemble mortality, RMST reads directly clinical scale, "many event-free time-units within \(\tau\)", usually one want report.

title: Plot a gg_partial_varpro object — plot.gg_partial_varpro
text: Ishwaran H, Kogalur UB, Blackstone EH, Lauer MS (2008). Random survival forests. Annals Applied Statistics, 2(3), 841–860. doi:10.1214/08-AOAS169 . Ishwaran H, Blackstone EH (2025). Harnessing power virtual (digital) twins: Graphical causal tools understanding patient hospital differences. Computational Structural Biotechnology Journal, 28, 312.

text: Breiman L. (2001). Random forests, Machine Learning, 45:5-32. Ishwaran H. Kogalur U.B. (2007). Random survival forests R, Rnews, 7(2):25-31. Ishwaran H. Kogalur U.B. randomForestSRC: Random Forests Survival, Regression Classification. R package version >= 3.4.0. https://cran.r-project.org/package=randomForestSRC

title: Predicted response plot from a gg_rfsrc object. — plot.gg_rfsrc
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- iris data # Build a small classification forest (ntree=50 keeps example fast) set.seed(42) rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_iris)  plot(gg_dta)   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Regression example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- air quality data # na.action = "na.impute" handles missing Ozone / Solar.R values set.seed(42) rfsrc_airq <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Ozone ~ ., data = airquality,                     na.action = "na.impute", ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_airq)  plot(gg_dta)   ## -------- mtcars data set.seed(42) rfsrc_mtcars <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars, ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_mtcars)  plot(gg_dta)   ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## Survival example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- veteran data ## randomized trial of two treatment regimens for lung cancer data(veteran, package = "randomForestSRC") set.seed(42) rfsrc_veteran <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ ., data = veteran, ntree = 50) gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran) plot(gg_dta)   # With 95% pointwise bootstrap confidence bands gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran, conf.int = .95) plot(gg_dta)   # Stratified by treatment arm gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_veteran, by = "trt") plot(gg_dta)   ## -------- pbc data (larger dataset -- skipped on CRAN) # \donttest{ data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC") # For whatever reason, the age variable is in days; convert to years for (ind in seq_len(dim(pbc)[2])) {   if (!is.factor(pbc[, ind])) {     if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {       if (sum(range(pbc[, ind], na.rm = TRUE) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }     }   } else {     if (length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 2) {       if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(0, 1)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }       if (sum(sort(unique(pbc[, ind])) == c(FALSE, TRUE)) == 2) {         pbc[, ind] <- as.logical(pbc[, ind])       }     }   }   if (!is.logical(pbc[, ind]) &     length(unique(pbc[which(!is.na(pbc[, ind])), ind])) <= 5) {     pbc[, ind] <- factor(pbc[, ind])   } } # Convert age from days to years pbc$age <- pbc$age / 364.24 pbc$years <- pbc$days / 364.24 pbc <- pbc[, -which(colnames(pbc) == "days")] pbc$treatment <- as.numeric(pbc$treatment) pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 1)] <- "DPCA" pbc$treatment[which(pbc$treatment == 2)] <- "placebo" pbc$treatment <- factor(pbc$treatment) # Remove test-set patients (those with no assigned treatment) dta_train <- pbc[-which(is.na(pbc$treatment)), ]  set.seed(42) rfsrc_pbc <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(   Surv(years, status) ~ .,   dta_train,   nsplit = 10,   na.action = "na.impute",   forest = TRUE,   importance = TRUE,   save.memory = TRUE )  gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc) plot(gg_dta)   gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, conf.int = .95) plot(gg_dta)   gg_dta <- gg_rfsrc(rfsrc_pbc, by = "treatment") plot(gg_dta)  # }

title: ROC plot generic function for a gg_roc object. — plot.gg_roc
code: ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## classification example ## ------------------------------------------------------------ ## -------- iris data # Build a small classification forest (ntree=50 keeps example fast) set.seed(42) rfsrc_iris <- randomForestSRC::rfsrc(Species ~ ., data = iris, ntree = 50)  # ROC for setosa (outcome index 1) gg_dta <- gg_roc(rfsrc_iris, which_outcome = 1) plot(gg_dta)   # ROC for versicolor (outcome index 2) gg_dta <- gg_roc(rfsrc_iris, which_outcome = 2) plot(gg_dta)   # ROC for virginica (outcome index 3) gg_dta <- gg_roc(rfsrc_iris, which_outcome = 3) plot(gg_dta)   # Plot all three ROC curves in one call by iterating over outcome indices n_cls <- ncol(rfsrc_iris$predicted) for (i in seq_len(n_cls)) print(plot(gg_roc(rfsrc_iris, which_outcome = i)))

title: Plot a gg_survival object. — plot.gg_survival
code: ## -------- pbc data data(pbc, package = "randomForestSRC") pbc$time <- pbc$days / 364.25  # This is the same as kaplan gg_dta <- gg_survival(   interval = "time", censor = "status",   data = pbc )  plot(gg_dta, error = "none")  plot(gg_dta)   # Stratified on treatment variable. gg_dta <- gg_survival(   interval = "time", censor = "status",   data = pbc, by = "treatment" )  plot(gg_dta, error = "none")  plot(gg_dta)  plot(gg_dta, label = "treatment")   # ...with smaller confidence limits. gg_dta <- gg_survival(   interval = "time", censor = "status",   data = pbc, by = "treatment", conf.int = .68 )  plot(gg_dta, error = "lines")  plot(gg_dta, label = "treatment", error = "lines") #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • fill : "treatment"   # ...with smaller confidence limits. gg_dta <- gg_survival(   interval = "time", censor = "status",   data = pbc, by = "sex", conf.int = .68 )  plot(gg_dta, error = "lines")  plot(gg_dta, label = "sex", error = "lines") #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • fill : "sex"
